blocks.text: Everything you need to set up ResearchOS, share a folder with your lab, and use each feature. Most users only need the first two sections, Getting Started and Shared Lab Accounts , to be productive.

blocks.title: First time here? Read this first.
blocks.text: The wiki is big because the app is big. Start Here covers the 7 things worth knowing up front and how to navigate the rest of the wiki.
60-second tour

ResearchOS is a single-page research-management app that reads and writes directly to a folder on your computer. There's no server, no account, no sign-up. Your data lives in plain JSON files you fully own.

items: Open ResearchOS in Chrome or Edge . Other browsers can't read local folders yet.

Click Link Folder to point ResearchOS at a folder on your disk. It can be an existing ResearchOS folder or a brand-new empty one you made first (Chrome cannot create a folder from the picker, so starting fresh means making an empty folder in your file manager, then linking it). For a quick intro first, BeakerBot offers an optional 3-minute walkthrough button in the upper-right of this screen. It's a 4-beat modal that explains data security and folder choice. It does not link a folder for you. See{" "} Connecting Your Folder {" "} for the full breakdown.
Pick a username. Your data lives under inside that folder. You can set an optional password to gate access on a shared machine.

Once you sign in, BeakerBot's in-product welcome tour auto-starts on the empty account. (This is the longer hands-on tour, separate from the optional 3-minute walkthrough on the folder picker.) Click through to learn the major surfaces. See Welcome Wizard for a breakdown of each step.
Start adding projects, methods, experiments, and Gantt tasks. Everything syncs to disk immediately.

blocks.title: Sharing a folder with your lab?
blocks.text: Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud lets multiple lab members use the same folder, but you have to configure it to stay downloaded locally . If you don't, ResearchOS won't be able to read and write reliably.
Browse by section
Where to find things

items: The ? icon in the top-right of the app header always opens this wiki.
On the folder-connect screen, look for the New here? Read the setup guide link, which goes to the Connecting Your Folder page.
Use Ctrl + F / Cmd + F to search within a wiki page.

Looking for security and privacy details? The Security page covers data storage, encryption, and what never leaves your machine. It does not appear in the section grid above because it has no sub-pages.

entries.title: Depositing to a repository
entries.summary: When a funder asks you to share your data, you publish it to a public repository that mints a DOI. ResearchOS does the tedious part for you. It gathers your experiment into a download-ready bundle and prefills the repository's metadata form from the grant and ORCID details you already entered, so the deposit takes minutes instead of an afternoon.
entries.blurb: Guided deposit to Zenodo or Figshare with prefilled DataCite metadata. You control the final publish.

blocks.text: When a funder asks you to share your data, you publish it to a public repository that mints a DOI. ResearchOS does the tedious part for you. It gathers your experiment into a download-ready bundle and prefills the repository's metadata form from the grant and ORCID details you already entered, so the deposit takes minutes instead of an afternoon.

A DOI (a permanent, citable link) is what turns a folder of files into shareable, findable research. Repositories like Zenodo and Figshare mint that DOI for you when you upload a dataset and fill in a metadata form. The form is the slow part, since it wants a title, an author with an ORCID, an abstract, a license, keywords, and the funder and award number behind the work. ResearchOS already holds most of that, so the Deposit flow fills the form in for you and hands you a clean bundle to upload.

blocks.title: How this fits the NIH policy
blocks.text: Depositing to a public repository is how you satisfy the sharing half of the NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy. For the full picture of what the policy requires and how ResearchOS supports it, see NIH Data Management & Sharing .
You always control the publish

This is the most important thing to understand about the Deposit flow. ResearchOS never publishes anything on your behalf and never talks to a repository for you. It builds the bundle and the metadata locally, in your browser, and hands them to you. The final upload and the final Publish button happen on the repository's own site, with your hands on the controls. The repository, not ResearchOS, mints the DOI.

That means nothing leaves your machine until you decide to upload it, and nothing becomes public until you click Publish on the repository. There is no auto-publish, no background sync, and no surprise release of your data.

blocks.title: One-click publishing is coming, not here yet
blocks.text: A future Phase 2 will offer one-click browser-direct publishing to Zenodo, whose deposit API works straight from the browser. That is on the roadmap and is not shipped today. Everything described on this page is the guided path that ships now, where you finish the upload on the repository yourself.
Opening Deposit
You can deposit a single experiment or a whole project.

items: From an experiment : open the experiment's detail popup and press Deposit to a repository . The bundle is that one experiment.
From a project : open the project and choose Deposit to bundle the project as a collection of its experiments.

blocks.text: The three steps
The Deposit dialog walks through curation, metadata review, and handoff.

Curate. Choose which sections and attachments go in the bundle, and pick the bundle format (a self-contained HTML page, a PDF, or the raw files). This reuses the same export pipeline that powers a normal experiment export, so the bundle looks exactly like the rest of ResearchOS's exports.
Review the metadata. ResearchOS prefills a DataCite-shaped metadata record (the standard repositories speak) and shows it for you to check and edit. See the field list below.

Hand off. Pick Zenodo , Figshare , or Other repository . ResearchOS downloads the data bundle plus a metadata file, then opens the repository's own upload page in a new tab. You drag the bundle in, paste or reference the metadata, and click Publish there to mint the DOI.

blocks.alt: The Deposit dialog on its metadata review step, showing the prefilled title, a creator row with an ORCID, an editable abstract, a license picker, keyword chips, and a funding reference block listing the funder name and award number.
blocks.caption: The metadata review step. Every field is prefilled from data you already entered and stays editable before you hand off.

blocks.text: What gets prefilled
The metadata step starts from what ResearchOS already knows, so you mostly confirm rather than type.

items: Title : the experiment title, or the project name for a project-level deposit.
Creator with ORCID : your display name, with your ORCID attached as a name identifier when you have one on file.
Abstract : seeded from your results, then notes, with the editing-history stamps stripped out. Fully editable.
License : you pick one before handoff. The two NIH-friendly defaults (CC-BY-4.0 and CC0-1.0) are surfaced first.
Keywords : pulled from the experiment's tags.

Funder and award number : read from the grant linked to the project, including the funder name, the funder ID and ID type, and the award number and title. These populate the DataCite so the deposit carries the grant attribution funders expect.

blocks.title: Where the funder data comes from
blocks.text: The funder fields come from the funding account linked to your project. You enter those structured grant details (award number, funder name, funder ID, award title) once in the funding-accounts manager, then link a project to that grant. See Purchases & Funding for how to set up the grant and the project-to-grant link.

blocks.title: The metadata mapping is test-covered
blocks.text: The translation from your ResearchOS data into the DataCite record is a pure, unit-tested function. Its tests assert the title, the creator ORCID, the funding reference, and the graceful fallbacks when a piece is missing, so the field mapping you see in the dialog is verified, not hand-wavy. That matters for a compliance artifact you are attaching your name to.
After the handoff

The deposit bundle and the metadata file are downloads. They are never written into your on-disk data folder and never become a new sidecar, so depositing leaves your project files untouched. Once the repository mints the DOI, copy it back into ResearchOS wherever you cite the dataset, and record it in your Data Management and Sharing Plan as your funder requires.

entries.title: ResearchOS vs LabArchives
entries.summary: An honest side-by-side. LabArchives is the incumbent most academic labs are leaving, so it is the right thing to measure against. ResearchOS wins decisively on data ownership and cost; LabArchives is still ahead on a few specific things, and this page says so plainly.
entries.blurb: An honest side-by-side: where ResearchOS wins, where LabArchives is still ahead.

blocks.text: An honest side-by-side. LabArchives is the incumbent most academic labs are leaving, so it is the right thing to measure against. ResearchOS wins decisively on data ownership and cost; LabArchives is still ahead on a few specific things, and this page says so plainly.
Who each one is for

LabArchives is a hosted, cloud ELN sold per seat. You trade money and data custody for managed backups, security certifications, and turnkey institutional administration. It suits labs that want a vendor to hold the data and an IT department that wants a certified cloud contract.

ResearchOS is a free, open-source, local-first ELN. Your data is a folder of open files on your own disk, shared across a lab through whatever cloud drive you already pay for. It suits labs that want to own their data outright, avoid per-seat fees, and keep unpublished work off a vendor server.
Where ResearchOS wins

items: You own your data, in open formats, forever. The single biggest difference. Your work is plain files on your disk, not rows in a vendor database. NIH's repository guidance prizes exactly this kind of portability and non-proprietary format.

No per-seat fees. A ten-person lab on LabArchives Professional is several thousand dollars a year, every year. ResearchOS has no per-seat fees and no paid tiers: it is free for every lab, whether you self-host or use the hosted version.
Unpublished research never leaves your control. No vendor server holds your pre-publication data. For sensitive or competitive work, that is a real advantage, not a checkbox.
Built for bench work. The visual PCR editor, plate layouts, LC gradients, cell-culture schedules, Gantt scheduling, and lab purchasing are first-class, not bolt-ons.
Switching in is easy. ResearchOS imports your LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP directly, so you are not retyping years of notebooks.

blocks.text: Where LabArchives is still ahead
Saying otherwise would not survive a demo, so here it is straight:

items: Fully automated one-click deposit. Built-in Figshare export and direct DOI minting from inside the app. ResearchOS has a guided deposit today (it prefills the metadata, bundles the data, and opens the repository's upload page where the DOI is minted), and the fully automated one-click publish is coming. See Depositing to a repository .

Complete per-entry revision history. ResearchOS now ships per-entry version history with a restore button on notes, tasks, and projects, so the gap here is narrow. LabArchives still covers every record type, while ResearchOS does not yet version results and methods.
Security certifications. FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, and 21 CFR Part 11. If your institution mandates a certified cloud vendor, this is decisive, and ResearchOS does not compete here by design.
Managed backups and any-browser access. The vendor handles redundancy, and there is no File System Access API browser requirement.

blocks.title: The honest bottom line
blocks.text: If your priorities are owning your data in open formats, eliminating per-seat cost, and keeping unpublished work off a vendor server, ResearchOS is the better choice and supports your NIH DMS compliance fully (see NIH Data Management & Sharing ). If your institution requires a certified cloud vendor, managed backups, or one-click DOI publishing as non-negotiables, LabArchives still wins on those specific points. Neither tool is "NIH certified," because no such certification exists.

entries.title: NIH Data Management & Sharing
entries.summary: Vendors advertise "NIH Data Management & Sharing support" like it is a certificate they bought. It is not. This page explains what the policy actually requires, why no electronic notebook is "NIH certified," and exactly how ResearchOS supports your compliance.
entries.blurb: What the policy actually requires, why there is no certification, and how ResearchOS supports it.

blocks.text: Vendors advertise "NIH Data Management & Sharing support" like it is a certificate they bought. It is not. This page explains what the policy actually requires, why no electronic notebook is "NIH certified," and exactly how ResearchOS supports your compliance.
There is no such thing as a certified ELN

The NIH Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Policy, in effect since January 25 2023, binds researchers and institutions that receive NIH funding. It does not bind software. There is no NIH program that audits, licenses, or certifies an electronic lab notebook, and there is no badge a tool can earn. When LabArchives or anyone else lists "NIH Data Management Mandates & Sharing support" as a feature, that is a marketing claim that decodes to one honest sentence: our features help your researchers do the things NIH expects of them .

blocks.title: So what are universities actually paying for?
blocks.text: Not a DMS certificate. The enterprise price tag buys security certifications (FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, 21 CFR Part 11), hosted backups, institutional administration, and brand trust. The "NIH selected LabArchives" line you may have seen refers to NIH's intramural program (NIH's own internal scientists), won through federal procurement on the strength of FedRAMP. A grantee lab at a university does not need any of that to comply with the DMS Policy.

Compliance is something a researcher does, not something a tool is. You comply by writing a Data Management and Sharing Plan with your grant application and then following it. The notebook is where the work that the plan describes actually happens. Any ELN with the right features can legitimately say it supports the policy, and ResearchOS has those features.
What the policy actually asks for
NIH expects funded investigators to do three things:

items: Plan and budget for managing and sharing scientific data, before the work starts.
Write a DMS Plan (a two-page document) and submit it with the funding application.
Implement the plan : manage the data rigorously during the project, then share it no later than publication or the end of the award, whichever comes first.

blocks.title: NIH does not want your lab notebook
blocks.text: This is the part most vendor pages bury. NIH explicitly says investigators are not expected to share laboratory notebooks, preliminary analyses, drafts, or communications. What you share is a curated dataset , deposited into an appropriate repository. So a notebook's real job is twofold: manage the data well while you work, and produce a clean, portable export you can hand to a repository when it is time to share. That plays directly to how ResearchOS stores everything.
How ResearchOS supports it

ResearchOS supports all three expectations. The same plan / create / implement structure other ELN vendors use to map their features to the policy applies here, with the bonus that your data lives in open formats on your own disk the entire time.
1. Plan and budget

Use separate projects to model how each grant's data will be organized, and reusable methods and templates so the whole lab captures data the same way from day one. Planning the structure up front is most of what a DMS Plan's data-organization section asks you to describe.
2. Create the DMS Plan

The plan itself is a short document built against NIH's required format. Most institutions point researchers at the free DMPTool , which has the official NIH template built in. Draft it there, then keep the finished plan in ResearchOS as a note or attachment alongside the project it governs, so the plan and the data it describes live together.
3. Implement the plan

This is where the day-to-day work lives, and where ResearchOS is strongest. The table below maps what the policy and NIH's Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories guidance expect against the ResearchOS feature that delivers it.
Where your data actually gets shared

The DMS Plan names a repository, and that repository is where sharing happens, not the notebook. NIH does not mandate a specific one. It publishes Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories (persistent identifiers, long-term stewardship, curation, clear reuse terms) and expects you to pick one that fits your data: a domain-specific repository where one exists, or a generalist repository such as Zenodo, Figshare, Dryad, or Vivli otherwise.

ResearchOS gets you to that handoff cleanly with a guided deposit dialog. You pick the repository (Zenodo, Figshare, or another of your choice), and ResearchOS prefills the deposit metadata in the DataCite shape repositories expect (title, creator with ORCID, abstract, license, keywords, and the funder and award pulled from the grant you linked), bundles the curated data, and opens that repository's own upload page so you drop the bundle in and the repository mints the DOI. Because your ResearchOS data is already in open formats, there is nothing to unpack or convert first. See Depositing to a repository for the step-by-step.
Honest limits worth knowing about

ResearchOS supports your DMS compliance, and it is honest about where a feature is a manual step rather than a built-in one. None of these block compliance, but you should know them before you write your plan.

blocks.title: Guided deposit now, one-click coming soon
blocks.text: ResearchOS has a guided deposit dialog today: it prefills your DataCite-shaped metadata, bundles the data, and opens the repository's own upload page, where the repository mints the DOI. What is not shipped yet is the fully automated one-click path, where ResearchOS publishes straight to Zenodo from the browser and the DOI comes back without you leaving the app. That is Phase 2. For now you finish the last step on the repository's page, so the deposit is guided rather than one-click.

blocks.title: Per-entry version history with restore ships today
blocks.text: Alongside the append-only PI audit log and soft-delete Trash, ResearchOS now keeps a full per-record version history with a restore button and a 24-hour undo on notes, tasks, and projects. You can browse prior versions, see who changed what, and roll an entry back. See Version history . Results and methods do not have it yet, so if your plan requires a complete versioned history of those two record types, account for that narrower gap now.

blocks.title: Structured grant and ORCID fields feed the deposit
blocks.text: ResearchOS has first-class fields for your ORCID iD (on your profile) and for funder and award numbers (on each funding account), named to match the DataCite schema a DOI deposit uses. The guided deposit dialog reads them automatically and prefills the metadata for you, so you confirm the details rather than retyping them at upload time. The remaining piece is the one-click publish that finishes the deposit without leaving the app.

blocks.title: No third-party security certifications
blocks.text: ResearchOS holds no FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, or 21 CFR Part 11 attestation. Its security posture is different by design: a local-first app with no vendor database to certify (see Security ). That is the right trade-off for many labs, but if your institution requires a certified cloud vendor, ask them which attestation they need.
The official sources
Do not anchor a compliance decision to a vendor blog (including this one). The primary sources are short and readable:

items: NIH DMS Policy overview and the policy hub at sharing.nih.gov .
NOT-OD-21-013 : the Final NIH Policy itself.
NOT-OD-21-014 : the required elements of a DMS Plan. This is the real checklist.
NOT-OD-21-016 : desirable characteristics of data repositories.

blocks.text: Wondering how ResearchOS stacks up against the tool most labs are leaving? See ResearchOS vs LabArchives .

entries.title: Calendar
entries.summary: A month, week, and day calendar for your conferences, deadlines, and meetings, with optional overlays from Google, Outlook, and iCloud.
entries.blurb: Native events plus external read-only feeds.

blocks.text: A month, week, and day calendar for your conferences, deadlines, and meetings, with optional overlays from Google, Outlook, and iCloud.

blocks.alt: The Calendar page in month view, with native event blocks and the +New Event button in the top right.

blocks.text: The Calendar page

The Calendar page lays out your ResearchOS events on a grid alongside any read-only items pulled in from a linked Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendar. Both kinds of event render with a filled colored background; the text color flips between black and white automatically so the title stays readable regardless of which color a feed picks. External events also carry a small link icon in the corner, which is the visual cue that the event came from a linked calendar rather than from a ResearchOS write.

The header strip holds the current date heading, prev/next arrows, a Today shortcut, and a month / week / day toggle. The two buttons on the far right are Linked Calendars (connect Google, Outlook, or paste an iCal URL) and + New Event .
Switch views

items: Month is a 7-column grid of day cells. Each cell shows up to three events, and the rest collapse into a count (a non-clickable label, not a link). Single-click a day to open a day-detail drawer listing every event on that date. Double-click to start a new event prefilled with that date.

Week is a 7-day stack with an all-day strip across the top and an hourly time grid below. Click any empty 15-minute slot in the grid to open the New Event popover prefilled with that date and time. A red "now" line tracks the current minute on today's column.
Day shows the same hourly grid for a single day, also with a red "now" line tracking the current minute. Useful first thing in the morning, and roomy enough to show an event's location inline.

blocks.title: Default view
blocks.text: The month/week/day toggle changes your view for the current session only. The change is ephemeral: when you reload the app, the calendar returns to its persisted startup view. To change that startup view, open Settings → View defaults and pick a different starting view. ResearchOS reads the setting from settings.json on load and hydrates the calendar to that view before rendering.
Create an event

blocks.title: Default colors by type
blocks.text: If you don't pick a color, the event uses its type's default: purple for conferences, red for deadlines, blue for meetings, gray for other.
Edit or delete an event

Click a ResearchOS event block to open its details popover. Edit swaps the popover into a form with every field, including a "Clear times (make all-day)" shortcut. Save writes the change. Delete removes the event after a confirmation.

blocks.title: Mark as PTO day
blocks.text: The event create/edit modal includes a Mark as PTO day checkbox. Events flagged PTO display a badge on their chip in the calendar grid. ResearchOS also factors PTO days into streaks and project schedule calculations, so marking time off keeps your progress tracking accurate.
Linked calendars (Google, Outlook, iCloud)

blocks.alt: The Linked Calendars modal showing the Connected list at top and an iCal URL form below.

blocks.text: Click Linked Calendars in the top right (or Manage in the sidebar) to open the modal. You can also navigate to directly, which auto-opens the modal so you can share a deep-link that drops someone straight into the add-feed flow. The same iCal-URL flow works for all four providers:

items: Google Calendar exposes a per-calendar Secret address in iCal format under Settings → Settings for my calendars. Copy that URL, paste it into the form.
Outlook / Microsoft 365 publishes a calendar from Settings → Calendar → Shared calendars → Publish a calendar. Copy the ICS link Outlook generates, paste it into the form.
iCloud / Apple Calendar shares via Calendar app → right-click → Share Calendar → Public Calendar. Paste the URL as-is. ResearchOS rewrites it automatically.
Other (any iCal URL) accepts any standard feed URL, so you can add lab-managed or third-party calendars that aren't one of the three named providers.

blocks.text: Every linked calendar gets a color in the sidebar legend. The Linked Calendars modal also has a per-feed color picker so you can recolor any feed to match your preferences without disconnecting and re-adding it. Toggle a feed on or off from either the modal or the legend. Remove a feed with the Remove link in the modal. Feeds refetch every 15 minutes. If a feed fails to fetch, an amber banner appears above the calendar grid with a Retry now button.

Clicking a linked event opens a read-only popover with the title, time, location, and any notes the source calendar published. To edit a linked event, open it in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar) and the change shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh.
Want the deep-dive? Calendar Feeds walks through per-provider setup with screenshots.

blocks.title: iCal URLs are sensitive
blocks.text: A public iCal URL grants read access to every event on that calendar to anyone who has the link. ResearchOS only stores it in your private data folder, but treat the URL itself like a password.
Reminders

Reminders are a single global preference, set from the Reminders link at the top of the calendar sidebar. The modal lets you turn reminders on, pick how far ahead you want to be notified (anywhere from 1 minute to 1 day before), and optionally grant browser-notification permission for an OS-level popup.

When a timed event's reminder fires, ResearchOS adds an entry to the bell inbox in the top bar. If you've granted browser permission, it also raises a system notification you can click to jump back to the tab. All-day events don't produce reminders, since there's no obvious moment to fire on.

blocks.title: A tab has to be open
blocks.text: Reminders run in JavaScript inside an open ResearchOS tab. If every tab is closed at the moment the reminder is due, the real-time popup won't fire, but the reminder is still waiting in the bell inbox the next time you open the app.
The Calendar sidebar
On the Calendar page the usual left sidebar swaps in a calendar-specific one with two sections:

items: Calendars lists ResearchOS events plus every linked feed, color-matched to the grid. Click a feed row to hide or show its events without disconnecting it. The Reminders and Manage links sit at the top of this section.
Upcoming shows the next 30 days, grouped by date (Today / Tomorrow / weekday + date) with up to 40 entries. Click any item to jump the main calendar to that day in day view.

entries.title: Cloning Workspace
entries.summary: The Cloning Workspace assembles new constructs in silico. Pick a chemistry, add your fragments, and review the product before it ever touches the bench.
entries.blurb: In-silico assembly: Gibson and NEBuilder overlap, Golden Gate, restriction-ligation, and Gateway, with a review step before anything is saved.

blocks.text: The Cloning Workspace assembles new constructs in silico. Pick a chemistry, add your fragments, and review the product before it ever touches the bench.

blocks.title: Split out of the Sequences page
blocks.text: This page used to be one section of the Sequences workbench page. The cloning chemistries grew their own depth, so they now live here. The Cloning Workspace is still launched from inside the Sequences library, and the products it makes land back in that same library.
What the Cloning Workspace is

Cloning is the act of joining DNA fragments into a new molecule. Before you order primers or set up a reaction at the bench, it helps to know exactly what the assembled construct will be, where each fragment lands, and which oligos you need. The Cloning Workspace is the tool that answers those questions without leaving ResearchOS. It is a pure in-silico assembly engine: you describe the reaction, and it computes the product sequence, the features carried across the junctions, and the primers (when a chemistry needs them), all in your browser.

The Workspace opens from the Assemble button in the header of the Sequences library, not from an editor tab or the top navigation. Inside, a single method picker offers four assembly chemistries, and every one of them runs the same calm two-step flow: pick and order your fragments and set the method's options, then review the computed product and save it to the library.

blocks.alt: The Cloning Workspace open over the Sequences library, showing the four method tabs (Overlap, Restriction, Golden Gate, Gateway) across the top, a fragments-in-order panel on the left, and the DNA library on the right.
blocks.caption: The Cloning Workspace. Pick a chemistry from the four method tabs, add fragments from your library or paste a sequence, then set the method's options.

blocks.text: The two-step flow

Every chemistry shares the same shape, so once you have done one assembly the rest feel familiar. The first step is the pick step. You choose the method, add the fragments or substrate molecules the reaction needs (from the library or pasted directly), order and orient them, and set whatever options the method exposes. The second step is the review step. The Workspace renders the assembled product with its features, the junctions it formed, and any warnings worth a second look (an internal cut site that the chosen enzyme would sever, an overlap that came out below target, an orientation-ambiguous ligation that yields more than one product). Saving the product creates a new sequence in the active collection, where it opens in the normal editor like any other sequence.
Overlap assembly (Gibson / NEBuilder HiFi)

Overlap assembly joins an ordered set of fragments that share short homologous ends. At the bench you amplify each fragment with primers whose 5' ends carry a homology tail matching the neighbouring fragment; the exonuclease chews back, the matching single strands anneal, and the fragments fuse seamlessly with the shared homology present exactly once at each junction. This is the chemistry behind Gibson assembly and NEBuilder HiFi.

In the Workspace you add two or more fragments in the order you want them joined, choose whether the product is linear or circular, and pick how the homology overlap is sized. You can fix the overlap to a length in base pairs (the default is 25 bp) or let the engine grow it until the homology reaches a target melting temperature. From there the Workspace does two things. It computes the assembled product as the fragment bodies joined in order, with the homology living once at each seam. And it designs the per-fragment PCR primers: each primer is an annealing region sized to a target Tm (about 60 degrees C, using the same nearest-neighbor model as the rest of the app) plus a 5' homology tail copied from the adjacent fragment's end, so the amplicons carry the overlap.

The designed oligos are yours to use directly. The review step offers them as a copyable oligo order list, or you can have them saved as features on the product so the primers travel with the template they belong to. The junction report flags any overlap that came out shorter than you asked for (a flanking fragment was too short) or any two junctions that share the same overlap sequence (the assembly may be ambiguous).

blocks.title: Orientation is yours to set
blocks.text: Overlap assembly takes each fragment in the orientation you supply it (top strand, 5' to 3', left to right). The engine does not silently reverse-complement a fragment to find a better junction, which keeps the result predictable. Order and orient the fragments the way the construct should read before you assemble.
Restriction and ligation

Restriction-ligation is the classic cut-and-paste of molecular cloning. A restriction enzyme cuts double-stranded DNA at a defined site, and when the two strands are cut at offset positions it leaves a single-stranded overhang (a sticky end). Two ends ligate when their overhangs are complementary and the same length, or when both ends are blunt. DNA ligase seals the strands and the overhang appears once, as the seam between the fragments.

In the Workspace you choose two or more sequences and a set of common cutters (EcoRI, BamHI, HindIII, PstI, KpnI, SmaI, XhoI, NotI, and others). The engine digests each sequence on both strands, types every resulting end as blunt, a 5' overhang, or a 3' overhang, and then enumerates the products whose ends form a consistent ligation chain. Because a fragment can ligate in either orientation, a pair of identical overhangs is genuinely ambiguous and yields more than one product. The Workspace returns every distinct product rather than guessing, so you pick the intended one in the review step.
Golden Gate (Type IIS)

Golden Gate assembly is a one-pot, scarless method built on Type IIS enzymes. Unlike a classic restriction enzyme, a Type IIS enzyme such as BsaI, BsmBI, BbsI, or SapI cuts outside and downstream of its recognition site. That means the recognition sequence ends up on a flanking piece that gets discarded, and the central part keeps a custom four-base overhang (three bases for SapI) with no scar left behind. Design the overhangs to be unique and the parts only fit together one way.

In the Workspace you add your parts and choose a Type IIS enzyme. The engine digests every part with that enzyme, drops the pieces that still carry the recognition site (the flanks), and ligates the central parts by their custom overhangs into a seamless product. The review step warns you if a kept piece has a blunt end, which usually means a part is missing a flanking Type IIS site.
Gateway (BP and LR)

Gateway cloning moves a DNA segment between vectors by site-specific recombination, with no restriction digestion and no ligation. The reaction happens between short att sites that share a common core: BP Clonase recombines an attB-flanked insert with an attP donor to make an attL entry clone (and an attR byproduct), and LR Clonase recombines an attL entry clone with an attR destination to make an attB expression clone (and an attP byproduct). The att sites carry a specificity number, and a site 1 recombines only with the partner's site 1, which is what makes Gateway directional.

In the Workspace you pick the reaction (BP or LR) and the two substrate molecules. The engine locates the att sites on each substrate by matching the published site sequences on both strands, checks that each substrate presents one site 1 and one site 2, and then computes each product att site as the true crossover recombinant of the two inputs. The gene of interest transfers onto the partner backbone and the cassette transfers out, exactly as in the wet-lab reaction. The result is the desired clone plus the byproduct, each a circular molecule with its features carried across.

blocks.title: Gateway substrate topology
blocks.text: Gateway substrates are normally supercoiled circles. The donor and destination vectors must be circular to recombine; the entry clone in an LR reaction should be circular too, while the attB-PCR product fed into a BP reaction can be linear. The review step warns you when an input topology would not recombine in the standard reaction.
Reviewing and saving the product

Whichever chemistry you used, the review step is the same surface. It shows the assembled product sequence with its features, the junctions it formed, and any warnings. Features from each input fragment are rebased into the product coordinates, so a CDS or a promoter that started on an input fragment lands at the right place on the new construct. When a chemistry designs primers, those appear here too, ready to copy or to save as features. When you accept the product, it is saved as a new sequence in the active collection and opens in the editor like any other sequence.

blocks.alt: The Cloning Workspace review step showing the recombinant construct sequence, a per-junction breakdown with primer overlaps and annealing temperatures, and a Save to library button.
blocks.caption: The review step. The recombinant construct, each junction's primers and annealing temperatures, and the oligo order list are shown before you save to your library.

blocks.title: The assembled construct is validated, not estimated
blocks.text: A wrong cloning product is a real molecular-biology error, so the assembly engines are not approximations. The overlap and cut-and-ligate products are cross-validated against the independent pydna simulator, and the Gateway crossover is validated against the published canonical att-site sequences. Those checks run as part of the test suite and are recomputed on every commit, and the comparison is published openly on the Transparency page .
Where the products go

A product saved from the Cloning Workspace is a first-class sequence in the Sequences library, filed under the active collection. It opens in the same editor, renders the same circular and linear maps, carries the features the assembly rebased onto it, and goes through the same trash flow as every other record if you delete it. The primers you designed during an overlap assembly, if you saved them as features, travel with the construct as annotations.

entries.title: The Workbench
entries.summary: The Workbench is where active research lives day to day. One page, four tabs (Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists), each with its own way of grouping work. The URL is /workbench, and /experiments still redirects there for backwards compatibility.
entries.blurb: Three tabs (Experiments, Notes, Lists) with a shared project filter, popup with Details / Lab Notes / Method / Results, PDF / HTML / Raw export.

blocks.text: The Workbench is where active research lives day to day. One page, four tabs (Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists), each with its own way of grouping work. The URL is /workbench, and /experiments still redirects there for backwards compatibility.

blocks.alt: The Workbench page with the Experiments tab active, showing the tab strip, the project filter pills, and the experiment cards.
blocks.caption: The Workbench with the Experiments tab selected (screenshot predates the kanban redesign).

blocks.text: Four tabs, four ways to slice the work

The Workbench packs four different views of your in-flight work into one page. The tab strip across the top names them and gives each a color identity: Projects (indigo), Experiments (blue), Notes (emerald), Lists (violet). The active tab fills with its color; the others read as gray text buttons. Projects is the default landing tab, so opening ResearchOS drops you on a grid of your projects rather than a flat task list (see Project Surface ).

Each tab uses a different organizing principle. The Projects tab is a browsable grid of project cards. The Experiments tab arranges protocols by where they sit in the run cycle on a kanban board (ready, blocked, running, awaiting writeup), with a results zone below it. The Lists tab groups list tasks by how close their dates are to right now (overdue, doing, upcoming, recently done, earlier). The Notes tab skips grouping entirely and offers a flat, search-driven list. So if you want to know "what can I run today," you reach for Experiments. If you want to know "what paperwork is overdue," you reach for Lists. If you want to find the meeting note from three weeks ago, you reach for Notes.

A fifth tab appears for some people. Lab heads, and any member who is in a 1:1, also see a role-relative Mentoring or Check-ins tab (rose), the shared advising surface for a lab-head and member pair. It is gated, so a solo user with no 1:1s never sees it.
The project filter pill strip

On the Experiments and Lists tabs, a row of colored pills sits below the tab strip, one per project, plus an All projects pill on the left. Click a pill to narrow the view to that project, and click it again (or pick a different one) to clear. The filter carries across those two tabs, so picking a project on Experiments and switching to Lists opens Lists already narrowed to the same project. That keeps a single research question coherent across the two views.

blocks.title: Projects and Notes skip the filter strip
blocks.text: The Projects tab hides the pills because the cards on it already are the projects. The Notes tab hides them because notes aren't tied to any single project. To scope the note grid, use the search box at the top of that tab and filter by content instead.
Standalone tasks with no project

Some tasks never get filed under a project: a one-off experiment you spin up in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot stays project-less on purpose. To move a task into or out of a project later, open it and use the project dropdown on the popup's Details tab. Alongside your real projects it offers a Standalone (no project) option: pick it to drop the task off every project, or pick a real project to file an orphan task back under it. On the Gantt timeline the project dropdown also carries a Standalone toggle so you can show or hide those orphan tasks while a specific project is in focus.
The Experiments tab

The Experiments tab arranges every experiment task in your library by stage of the protocol. The in-flight work sits on a kanban board of four columns across the top, and the finished work sits in two results zones below it. The four board columns run side by side so you can scan the whole pipeline at a glance:
Ready to start
Experiments scheduled to begin today or recently, with no incomplete upstream task blocking the start. These are the cards you can pick up and run today, so the column reads first on the left.
Blocked

Experiments that depend on an upstream task that hasn't finished yet. The blocking parent's name renders directly under the card in a small amber strip so you know what you're waiting on. Click the parent name to jump straight to that task and unblock the chain.
Running

Experiments where today falls between the start date and the end date. Each card carries a Day N of M freshness label, and (if the experiment is part of a chain) a gray Next: pointer to the experiment that follows it so you can step through a multi-day protocol without leaving the Workbench.
Awaiting writeup

Experiments marked complete but with no results body or attached images on disk yet. This column is your reminder pile, the bench work is done but the writeup is still owed. When the column is empty it shows a bordered emerald chip reading "All recent experiments have results logged" in place of cards.

blocks.title: Each column carries its own count
blocks.text: Every board column has an uppercase header with a live count and a small info icon. Hover the icon for a one-line reminder of the rule that puts a card there (for Running, "Today falls between start and end date"). When all four columns are empty the board collapses to a single quiet "No in-flight experiments" line, and the results zones below still render.
Recent results

Below the board, the Recent results zone holds completed experiments with results landed in the last 30 days, in a wide full-card grid. When you have results across two or more projects, the zone sub-groups them by project (a small colored dot per project header) so a busy month doesn't collapse into one undifferentiated grid.
Earlier results

The archive at the bottom, completed experiments older than 30 days. The zone header carries a Flat / By project toggle so you can flip between a single time-ordered grid and one block per project. There's no time cap, so the whole completion history is reachable from one place. Long archives page in behind a Show more control, and by-project groups start collapsed.

blocks.alt: The Experiments tab showing the four-column kanban board (Ready to start, Blocked, Running, Awaiting writeup) with cards underneath each column.
blocks.caption: The four board columns run side by side, each with an uppercase header and a count (screenshot predates the kanban redesign).

blocks.text: Cards and chains

Each card carries the experiment name, a project pill, the project color along the edge, a hero image (if the writeup has one), a method chip strip, and a freshness label keyed to the column it sits in. When an experiment is part of a dependency chain, the Running card shows a gray Next: pointer to the experiment that follows it, and a blocked card shows the parent it's waiting on. The Gantt page is where you walk a chain step-by-step.
+ New Experiment
The blue button at the top right of the tab opens the New Task modal with the type pre-selected to experiment . It is the same modal you'd get from the Gantt.
The Notes tab

The Notes tab holds every note that isn't tied to a specific experiment. Meeting notes from a Tuesday lab meeting, a running log of equipment-room weirdness, half-formed ideas you want to find again next month: they all land here, in one flat list with no section headers. The choice to skip grouping is deliberate. Notes are search-first, not date-first, because a researcher coming back to a note usually remembers what the note was about, not when it was written.

blocks.alt: The Notes tab showing a search box, three type filter buttons (All / Single / Running Logs), the New Note button, and a grid of note cards.
blocks.caption: The Notes tab leads with a search box and three type filters; the grid below filters live as you type.

blocks.text: The header carries a search box on the left and three type filter buttons in the middle ( All , Single , Running Logs ). Single notes are one-page records of a moment (a meeting, an idea). Running logs are timestamped multi-entry pages you append to over time (an equipment journal, a daily standup log). The New Note button on the right opens a dropdown that asks which kind to create.

blocks.title: When to use Notes vs Lab Notes
blocks.text: The Notes tab is for writing that isn't attached to any single experiment. Per-experiment lab notes (running observations, what reagents you grabbed, the photo at the bench five minutes ago) live inside that experiment's popup on its Lab Notes tab. If the writing belongs to a protocol, it goes there. If it belongs to the lab, it goes here.
The Lists tab

The Lists tab is the queue view of every list task you own. Lists are the non-experiment task type: checklists, paperwork, ordering tasks, errands, anything that isn't a protocol. The Lists tab sorts them into five stages by how their dates relate to today, stacked top-to-bottom in this order:

items: Overdue : list tasks whose end date has passed and that aren't complete yet. The first thing in your queue every morning.
Doing : list tasks where today falls between start and end.

Upcoming : list tasks scheduled to start within the next 14 days. Tasks starting further out are omitted from the main list; a small gray footnote below the section reads "+ N scheduled later than 14d out" so you know they exist without cluttering the view.
Recently done : completed in the last 30 days.
Earlier : completed more than 30 days ago. Collapsed by default; click the header to expand.

blocks.text: Each card carries the task name, a project color dot with the project name, and a date signal phrased relative to today ("3d overdue", "Started yesterday", "Starts in 4d"). Clicking a card expands it inline as an accordion panel. Opening one card collapses the previously-open one.

blocks.alt: The Lists tab with five stacked sections (Overdue, Doing, Upcoming, Recently done, Earlier), each with list-task cards underneath.
blocks.caption: The Lists tab buckets list tasks into five date-relative stages, top to bottom.

blocks.text: Expanding a list task inline
Clicking a list card expands it in place. A violet border highlights the open card, and a panel slides down below the header row. Inside the panel:

items: Name row : click the name to rename it inline. Press Enter or click away to save.
Sub-task checklist : tick individual items to check them off. Click an item's text to rename it. The delete icon appears on hover to remove an item.
Add item : type in the input and press Enter (or click Add ) to append a new item to the checklist.
Mark list complete button in the footer: marks the whole list done and cascades all un-checked items to complete. Click again to mark it incomplete.
Open full view link in the footer: opens the legacy full-screen popup for the same task, useful for the Details and date-editing fields not exposed in the inline panel.

blocks.title: Lists holds list tasks, not experiments
blocks.text: Experiment tasks always render on the Experiments tab, never on Lists, even when their dates would make them overdue or upcoming. The split is by task type, not by status. The Lists tab is for the to-do work that supports your protocols, not the protocols themselves.
Opening an experiment: the four-tab popup

blocks.alt: An open experiment popup with the four tabs (Details, Lab Notes, Method, Results) visible across the top and the Details tab active.
blocks.caption: The popup opens on the Details tab by default. Use the fullscreen icon in the header to expand it to the whole window.

blocks.text: Clicking any experiment card opens a centered modal. The popup always opens on the Details tab, regardless of whether the experiment is ready, running, awaiting writeup, or completed. You land on the same view every time, then pick the tab that fits the task at hand.
The popup carries four tabs across the header:

items: Details is the entry tab. It holds the name, dates, duration, project, tags, and the high-level toggles. If the task carries a (legacy) deviation log string, it renders here in an amber box.
Lab Notes is the during-the-run markdown body, with a bottom attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) for photos, PDFs, and other files.
Method shows one tab per attached method, with the Variation Notes panel and (for PCR methods) an editable gradient and recipe.
Results is the second markdown editor, for the final writeup (the gel image you'd put in a paper, the plot for a thesis chapter), with its own attachment strip.

blocks.text: The header row also holds a green completion checkmark, an Export icon (down arrow, experiments only), a Share button, a fullscreen toggle, and Delete. Drag any file onto the popup and it lands in the last-viewed editor tab's or folder, with a confirmation toast at the drop point.
Lab Notes and Results

The two markdown tabs hold different kinds of writing on the same experiment. Lab Notes is the during-the-run record: running observations, what reagents you grabbed, what went sideways, the photo you took at the bench five minutes ago. Results is the final-output writeup: the gel image, the plot, the short summary of what the experiment actually showed. Both live on the same task and both write to the same per-task folder on disk, so flipping between the two tabs in the popup is instantaneous.

Both tabs use the same editor surface: a single live markdown body with an Edit / Preview toggle, an attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) pinned below the editor, and an Add File toolbar button. Saving is explicit: each Save checkpoint records a revertible version you can browse and restore from the Version history button. The full editor reference (shortcuts, the language picker, image-resize popovers, the attachment strip, checkpoints) lives on its own page: The Markdown Editor .

Drag images straight into the body to place them inline, or drop them on the strip to attach without inlining. Switch the strip to its Files tab for non-image attachments (PDFs especially); click a file link in the prose to open or download it.

blocks.title: Where the files live
blocks.text: Lab Notes saves to and Results saves to . Attachments split per-tab into , , , and . Files attached through the old separate Files panel ( / ) still show up in the unified Files tab, so nothing you attached before the strip went live is lost.
Attach a method (or a PCR protocol)
Variation Notes and per-run PCR tweaks

Each attached method has a collapsible amber Variation Notes panel at the top of its tab. Click + Add Note and the editor prepends a fresh heading so each entry is timestamped. Hover any rendered entry to reveal an in-place delete button. The notes save back to the experiment, not the method, so the shared method file stays clean.

For PCR-typed methods, the gradient editor and recipe table below the variation panel are editable per-experiment. Reset to Method drops your overrides back to the protocol's defaults, and Save Changes writes them to this task only.
The deviation log

Sometimes a run drifts from the protocol in a way worth recording on its own, not just a tweak but a real deviation. When a task carries a deviation note it renders in an amber box on the experiment's Details tab, so the next person to read the experiment sees exactly what was different.
The Note Deviations dialog is where you write one. You describe what changed during the run, then choose what to do with it:

items: Save to task results only. The deviation is recorded in this task's log and the underlying method stays untouched. Use this for a one-off change that only mattered for this run.

Fork as new method. The deviation becomes the seed of a brand-new method file, created as a child of the original with the deviation baked into its text. Use this when the change is good enough to keep as its own protocol going forward.

blocks.title: Save the run, or grow a new protocol
blocks.text: Reach for Save to task results only when the deviation belongs to this one experiment. Reach for Fork as new method when you've found a better recipe and want it in the Method Library for next time.
Export an experiment

Click the down-arrow icon in the popup header (tooltip: Export experiment ) to open the Export dialog. The dialog heading reads Export <experiment name> and lists three format cards, each with a short description of what you get and who it's for. Click a card and the file downloads straight to your browser's Downloads folder.

blocks.alt: The Export dialog open over an experiment popup, showing three format cards: PDF report, HTML report, and Raw ResearchOS format.
blocks.caption: The Export dialog. Pick a format card to start the download; close with Cancel or the Escape key.

items: PDF report : a professional, printable PDF with a title page, a clickable table of contents, an outline pane (bookmarks) for jumping between sections, and inline images. Lab Notes, Results, every attached method (rendered, with per-run PCR tweaks), Sub-tasks, the Deviation log, and a Files attached appendix all live in the same document. Text is selectable, so you can copy passages straight out into a thesis chapter, IRB filing, or grant report.

HTML report : a single self-contained page bundled in a with its attachments. Images are base64-inlined so the page renders even offline; PDF methods and other non-image files sit alongside in , , and . Open it in any browser, or mail the zip to a collaborator who doesn't run ResearchOS.

Raw ResearchOS format : the full experiment as a sharable bundle ( , , the raw and , each method as its own JSON file including PCR protocols, plus every attached image and file in the same folder layout the app uses on disk). This is the format another ResearchOS user imports through Settings → Import experiment to land the experiment in their own folder with all the methods, PCR protocols, and attachments wired up.

blocks.text: While the file builds, the dialog shows a Preparing export… spinner and disables every button (Escape and the backdrop are also locked) so you can't kick off a second export by accident. If anything goes wrong, an alert shows the error and the dialog stays open for a retry.

blocks.title: Pick the format by where the file is going
blocks.text: PDF for thesis chapters, IRB filings, lab-meeting handouts, and anything destined for print. HTML for sharing a polished read-only report with a collaborator who works outside ResearchOS. Raw for archival, lab handoff, or when another ResearchOS user is going to import the experiment into their own folder.

blocks.title: Exporting more than one at a time
blocks.text: The Export icon on an experiment popup is single-experiment only. To bundle a batch of experiments into one download, use Select on the Search page. Tick the result cards you want, click Export selected , and the same dialog packages every chosen experiment into an with one entry per experiment.

blocks.title: Sub-tasks live on list tasks, not experiments
blocks.text: The sub-task checklist you may have seen on the Home page belongs to the list task type, not experiment . Experiment popups don't carry a sub-task list. If you want a checklist alongside an experiment, make a separate list task and link it as a dependency.

entries.title: Feedback
entries.summary: The feedback button is a thin layer over GitHub Issues. Click it, pick a type (bug, feature, feedback), fill in the modal, and ResearchOS opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. Nothing submits automatically: you see the body, you edit it, you click Submit. The whole loop is the most privacy-respecting bug-tracking flow we could think of.
entries.blurb: Bug, feature, and feedback reports via pre-filled GitHub issues.

blocks.text: The feedback button is a thin layer over GitHub Issues. Click it, pick a type (bug, feature, feedback), fill in the modal, and ResearchOS opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. Nothing submits automatically: you see the body, you edit it, you click Submit. The whole loop is the most privacy-respecting bug-tracking flow we could think of.

blocks.alt: The FeedbackModal with the Bug radio selected, an editable Title field, an editable description, and an Auto-attached error details section at the bottom.
blocks.caption: The FeedbackModal with Bug selected. The body is editable; nothing leaves your machine until you click through to GitHub.

blocks.text: Where the button lives

Every page in ResearchOS carries a FeedbackButton in the bottom toolbar (or the corresponding floating cluster on dashboards). Click it and the FeedbackModal mounts. The button is always available because every surface in the app is a fair target for feedback.
The three feedback types
Pick a type at the top of the modal:

items: Bug. Something is broken: a crash, a wrong number, a misrendering. The modal auto-attaches the current route, the browser/OS string, and any recent uncaught error details so the report has enough context for a fix.
Feature. A new capability you wish existed. The modal walks a lighter template (what you want, why) and routes to the feature-request label on GitHub.
Feedback. Anything that does not fit the first two: a comment on UX, a typo, a confused-by-naming note. Routes to the feedback label.

blocks.text: Auto-capture, no auto-submit

On submit, the modal does not POST to a server. It builds a GitHub issue URL with the title, body, and label pre-filled in the query string, then opens that URL in a new tab. You see the body GitHub is about to create, you can edit any of it, and the issue does not exist until you click Submit on the GitHub side. This means:

items: Nothing on your machine moves until you intentionally submit.
You can sanitize anything that landed in the auto-attached error details before posting.
The full report shape is determined by in the repo, so the GitHub side renders a templated form rather than a raw markdown body.

blocks.title: feedback.yml routing
blocks.text: The GitHub Issues template at defines the form fields and label routing on the receiving side. The modal sends a type query parameter that GitHub uses to pre-select the matching template. The result is one consistent shape regardless of who clicked Bug, Feature, or Feedback.
The BugStomp scene

The BugStomp scene (a small BeakerBot moment you might hit during onboarding or after a crash recovery) ends with the same feedback flow. Stomping the bug opens the FeedbackModal pre-set to Bug type and prefilled with a hint from the crash context. It is the same modal as the manual entry point; the only difference is the auto- populated body.

blocks.title: Why a pre-filled URL instead of a POST
blocks.text: We could have POSTed feedback straight to a webhook or a server we run, and the user would not have to see the body before submission. We did not, because the moment a POST endpoint exists, every typed word is a thing that left your machine before you saw the final copy. The pre-filled URL pattern keeps the auditable boundary crisp: nothing leaves until you click Submit on the GitHub page. See Security for the broader no-server posture this fits into.

entries.title: Gantt Chart
entries.summary: The Gantt timeline holds every active task across every project you can see. The view trades calendar precision for visual rhythm: you scan packed rows of colored bars and your eye picks out the busy weeks, the dependencies that cross projects, and the gaps where work is not yet planned. The chart is a working surface, not a read-only report. You drag bars to reschedule, drop one on another to chain them, right-click a day to mark it as PTO, and drop an image straight onto a task.
entries.blurb: Drag to reschedule, dependencies cascade automatically.

blocks.text: The Gantt timeline holds every active task across every project you can see. The view trades calendar precision for visual rhythm: you scan packed rows of colored bars and your eye picks out the busy weeks, the dependencies that cross projects, and the gaps where work is not yet planned. The chart is a working surface, not a read-only report. You drag bars to reschedule, drop one on another to chain them, right-click a day to mark it as PTO, and drop an image straight onto a task.

blocks.alt: The Gantt timeline with packed rows of colored bars, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, PTO stripes on a Saturday cell, and a dependency stripe linking two bars across rows.
blocks.caption: The Gantt at a glance: packed rows of bars colored by project, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, weekend hash, and PTO stripes.

blocks.text: Anatomy of the Gantt
Every visual element on the Gantt encodes one specific thing. Once the vocabulary clicks, a scroll across the timeline reads almost like sheet music.

items: One colored bar per task. Bar color is the project's color. Bar start and length are the task's start date and duration. Tasks pack into rows automatically (the chart is not grouped by project), so non-overlapping tasks share a row to keep the chart dense.

Dependency stripes. A short top stripe in a contrasting color marks tasks in a dependency chain. Every bar in the same chain shares the same stripe color, so you can trace a chain visually across rows. Standalone tasks show a dimmer per-experiment stripe.

Gradient bars for dependent tasks. When a bar is downstream of another (FS, SS, or SF), the fill renders as a subtle gradient instead of a flat color. The gradient direction mirrors the dependency direction, so you can read whether a task waits on something earlier or constrains something later.
Type icons. A horizontal-lines icon on the right plus a thicker white left edge marks a list task. A dollar-sign icon and a diagonal stripe mark a purchase task.
High-level tasks. Render in muted amber and sit at the top of the packed rows so they read as overlays on the regular schedule.
Completion state. Completed tasks desaturate and go partially transparent with a checkmark on the right edge. A partial fill on the left of a bar shows sub-task completion progress.
Shared badges. A small bracketed initial like on a bar means this task is shared. The bar still uses the project's color, not the owner's.
Edge fade. Bars that start before or end after the visible window fade to black on that edge to signal that they continue offscreen.
PTO stripes. Days you have marked as PTO render with sky-blue diagonal stripes plus a small dot in the day header. Cascades skip these days when computing dependent task dates.

Weekend hash. Saturday and Sunday columns read as light grey background. Tasks belonging to a 5-day project overlay weekend portions with a diagonal hash so you know work does not actually happen on those days. A 7-day project draws solid across weekends.

blocks.text: The toolbar

The toolbar at the top of the page is where you scope and zoom the chart. The 2026-05 redesign collapsed the old per-project pill row into a single multi-select dropdown so a lab with many projects can keep the chrome compact.

items: Projects dropdown (multi-select). Click it to open the project list. Every project starts checked, so the default chart shows everything. Untick a project to hide its bars. Two quick actions at the top: Select all restores the everyone-checked default; Unselect all clears the list so you can re-tick only the project you want.
Tag chips. When any task or project has tags, a row of tag chips appears next to the project dropdown. Toggle a chip to narrow the chart to tasks matching that tag.
Shared toggle. A small share-icon switch that hides or shows tasks others have shared with you.

Zoom segmented control. Eight buttons: , , , , , , , . Shorter zooms keep day-level detail; longer ones span many weeks. All currently shows an 8-week window starting from the current Monday, not a full-history view.
Week navigator on the right. Step the visible window back and forward one week at a time, with a date picker for jumping to a specific Monday and a Today link that snaps back to this week.
+ Task and + Goal buttons at the far right of the toolbar to add a task or a high-level goal without leaving the chart.

blocks.text: Reschedule by dragging

Grab any bar and drag it to a different day. On drop, the task's start date moves to that day and its duration stays the same. Any downstream tasks linked through a dependency recompute their dates to keep their constraints valid: an FS (start-after) child of a moved parent slides forward to start the day after the new parent end.

Most cascades apply silently. The orange "This change will affect N task(s)" modal only opens when an SF (finish-before) chain would shift a task to start in the past. The modal lists every affected task with its old and new dates and a per-task warning line for the past-date conflicts. Apply Changes commits the cascade; Cancel leaves the chain alone.

blocks.title: Weekend rules ride along
blocks.text: Dropping on a Saturday or Sunday in a project that has weekends disabled bumps the start date to the next Monday. Toggle the weekend setting from each project's detail popup on the Home page.
Chain two tasks with a dependency

A dependency captures the idea that one piece of bench work cannot sensibly begin (or end) until another one reaches a certain point. On the Gantt you express that by dragging one bar and dropping it on top of another. A popup then asks how the two tasks should be linked:

items: Start at same time (SS). Both tasks begin on the same day. On the chart they line up at the same left edge.
Start after (FS). The dragged task starts the day after the target finishes, so the two sit back to back with no overlap.

Finish before (SF). The dragged task finishes the day before the target starts. This is a strict gap: the predecessor clears the calendar entirely before the successor begins, so the two never share a day even when the predecessor is a single-day task.

blocks.text: Once you pick a relationship, the dragged task's start date snaps to whatever the dependency requires. Tasks in the same chain share the same colored top stripe, so you can trace a chain across bars. Drag a bar in the chain and downstream tasks shift with it.

blocks.title: Dependency chains are experiment-only
blocks.text: Only experiments can be chained. Lists and purchases are deliberately kept out of dependency chains, since linking a shopping order or a checklist into a scheduling chain tends to create constraints nobody wanted. On the Gantt this shows up as a non-interactive signal: drag a list or purchase bar (or aim one at a list or purchase target) and the blue drop zone never appears, so the link simply will not form. The same rule holds when you build a task: the "After Task" scheduling mode and its parent picker only surface for experiment tasks.
See a whole chain at once: hover-highlight

Because bars pack into rows by date rather than by chain, the members of one dependency chain can end up scattered across the timeline. Two cues help you read them as a group. The first is always on: each bar in a chain carries small chain-colored dots in its corner, where the dot count matches the chain length and the fully-opaque dot marks this task's position in the order.

The second cue is the hover treatment. Move your cursor over any bar that belongs to a multi-member chain and the whole chain lights up: every bar in that chain stays at full strength and picks up a ring in the chain's color, while every bar outside the chain dims back so the group reads as one unit. Move off and the chart returns to normal. Solo tasks (a chain of one, with no peers) do not trigger the effect.
Filter with the multi-select project dropdown
The redesigned toolbar replaced the long pill row with a single dropdown. The interaction is:

items: Click Projects to expand the list.
The default is all checked . The chart shows every project until you uncheck something.
Select all at the top of the dropdown restores the everyone-checked default (useful after a long Unselect-and-pick session).
Unselect all clears every checkmark in one click, so you can quickly re-tick only the one or two projects you want to focus on.
The dropdown stays open while you click, so you can stage the right filter set without dropdown-bounce.

blocks.text: Standalone tasks have no project to filter by

Not every task belongs to a project. A quick experiment you spin up without filing it under a research question lives in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot, with no project attached. Those orphan tasks used to vanish the moment you scoped the chart to a specific project, since they have no project key to match against.

The bottom of the Projects dropdown now carries a Standalone row (set off by a dashed divider and marked with a hollow dashed swatch and a small "no project" hint) whose tooltip reads "Toggle visibility of experiments with no project." Tick it to fold your standalone tasks back into the view alongside whatever projects you have checked, or untick it to hide them while you concentrate on real projects. It composes with the project checkboxes rather than replacing them, so you can show one project plus your standalone bucket at the same time.

To move a task into or out of that bucket, open the task and use the project dropdown in its detail popup. Alongside your real projects it lists a Standalone (no project) option; picking it drops the task off every project, and picking a real project files an orphan task back under it.
Gantt for PIs

PIs land on the same Gantt UI as every member, but the timeline pulls from a wider pool. The project dropdown on the left of the toolbar now spans every member's projects across the lab, not just the PI's own. One scroll of the chart shows the whole lab's work side by side.

This shifts the role of the page. For a member, the Gantt is a personal schedule. For a PI, it becomes a coordination surface: dependency chains can stretch across users, the project dropdown doubles as a per-member filter (untick a student's projects to focus on a co-PI's timeline), and rescheduling a bar cascades downstream tasks regardless of who owns them. The interaction model stays identical, but the decisions you make on the chart land on more people at once.

blocks.alt: The Gantt as a PI, with the project dropdown open showing projects from every member in the lab.
blocks.caption: Lab head view: the project dropdown spans every member's projects, so one timeline shows the whole lab.

blocks.text: Drop an image on a task

Drag any image file from Finder or the desktop and drop it on a task bar. The image attaches to that task as a new Images-folder entry and a markdown reference is appended to the task's Lab Notes body. This is the fastest path to file a gel image or a microscope snap straight from the chart, without first opening the task popup. The drop zone highlights blue while you are hovering, so you can tell which bar will receive the file.
Mark a day as PTO

Right-click any day cell in the date grid to open a context menu with a Mark as PTO / Unmark PTO option. Marked days render with sky-blue diagonal stripes and a small dot in the day header so they stand out at a glance. When a cascade computes new dates for chained tasks, PTO days are skipped, so dependent tasks slide past your time off automatically.
PTO marks are per-user (your time off is private), so a labmate looking at the same chart will not see your stripes unless they are viewing through a PI dashboard that aggregates across members.
Goals on the timeline

Goals appear in two places. Above each week's day headers, every active goal that overlaps the week renders as a thin colored bar (longer goals sit lower). Hover one for a tooltip showing the goal name and days remaining (or overdue). Click a bar to open the goal editor.

The Goal sidebar on the right lists active goals as cards. Each card surfaces SMART subgoals as checkboxes so you can tick them off without leaving the chart. Use the + Goal button in the toolbar to add a new high-level goal.
Open a task

Click a bar to open the task detail popup, where you can edit name, dates, tags, attached methods, notes, results, and subtasks. The popup is the same one used by the Workbench and the calendar, so changes here sync everywhere.

blocks.alt: A task detail popup opened over the Gantt chart, with Details, Lab Notes, Method, and Results tabs.
blocks.caption: Clicking a bar opens the full task editor over the Gantt. Same popup as the Workbench and the calendar.

entries.title: Where you land
entries.summary: Opening ResearchOS drops you straight into the surface that fits your role. There's no separate home dashboard to build or maintain. A lab member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench, opened to a grid of their projects. A lab head lands on the curated Lab Overview. The root URL is just a router that sends you to the right place.
entries.blurb: Members land on the Workbench Projects grid; PIs on the curated Lab Overview. No customizable home dashboard.

blocks.text: Opening ResearchOS drops you straight into the surface that fits your role. There's no separate home dashboard to build or maintain. A lab member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench, opened to a grid of their projects. A lab head lands on the curated Lab Overview. The root URL is just a router that sends you to the right place.
The root URL is a router, not a page

ResearchOS used to open on a customizable widget dashboard at : a canvas of tiles you pinned, dragged, and rearranged. That dashboard is gone. The root URL now renders nothing of its own. It signs you in, handles any deep link you arrived with (e.g. a task or project link someone shared), and then forwards you to the surface that owns your account type.

items: A lab member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench , opened to its Projects grid.
A lab head (PI) lands on the Lab Overview , the fixed curated page that rolls up the whole lab.

blocks.title: No dashboard to curate
blocks.text: There's no add-widget palette, no drag-to-arrange canvas, and no per-user dashboard layout to keep in sync anymore. Everyone gets one designed landing surface for their role, and the same{" "} Daily Tasks sidebar runs alongside it.
Members land on the Projects grid

For a member or solo researcher, the landing surface is the Workbench with its Projects tab selected. The grid shows a card per project with the project's color dot, a percent-complete progress bar, and the open-task count. Click a card to open that project's dedicated route (see Project Surface for the Overview, Results, Methods, Goals, and Activity walkthrough). The Workbench's other tabs (Experiments, Notes, Lists) sit alongside Projects in the same view.
Create a project
The New Project button lives on the Projects grid. It opens the same create modal everywhere a project can be started.

blocks.title: Color is visual grouping everywhere
blocks.text: The color you pick follows the project across the whole app: the bar on the Gantt , the dot on the project card, and the overlay on the calendar. Pick distinct colors early so a busy Gantt stays readable as your project count grows.
Shared projects appear here too

When a labmate shares a project with you, it surfaces in your Projects grid alongside your own. The card reads the same as any other: color, progress, open-task count. Whether your edits save back depends on the permission the owner granted: edit permission writes to their copy, view permission is read-only. See Sharing and permissions for the full permission model.

blocks.title: Want a lab-wide roll-up?
blocks.text: Sharing a single project is the right tool when one labmate wants to follow along on one specific project. For a single surface that rolls up every member's activity, today's events, and pending approvals at once, a PI uses the curated Lab Overview instead.

entries.title: Image annotation
entries.summary: Circle a gel band, arrow a feature on a micrograph, label the lanes. The image annotation editor lets you mark up a photo right where it lives in your notes, and it does so without ever touching the original file. Your markup is a separate, re-editable layer you can change or remove at any time.
entries.blurb: Mark up gels and micrographs without touching the original image. Annotations live in a sidecar you can delete to revert.

blocks.text: Circle a gel band, arrow a feature on a micrograph, label the lanes. The image annotation editor lets you mark up a photo right where it lives in your notes, and it does so without ever touching the original file. Your markup is a separate, re-editable layer you can change or remove at any time.

Lab images carry meaning that the pixels alone do not. A reviewer needs to know which band is the product, which colony you picked, which cell in the field is the one you counted. ResearchOS gives you a drawing surface for exactly that, and it treats your raw image as something you never want corrupted.
