{`your-folder/ ├── users/ │ ├── / │ │ ├── projects/ │ │ ├── tasks/ │ │ ├── methods/ │ │ ├── notes/ │ │ ├── Images/ │ │ └── settings.json │ └── public/ ← shared methods & protocols └── lab/ ← shared funding accounts`}
Everything is plain JSON and plain image files. You can back up the folder by copying it, version-control it with git, or open it in Finder / Explorer at any time.
Reconnecting later

After your first connect, ResearchOS remembers the folder name via browser storage. On your next visit, instead of the Link a folder card you see a dedicated reconnect screen : it shows the folder name in a bold heading ( Reconnect to [your-folder] ) and a prominent Continue button that re-attaches without reopening the OS picker. A smaller Pick a different folder link below the button lets you switch to a different location if needed. The browser may show a one-time permission prompt the first time you reconnect after a browser restart.

blocks.title: Clearing site data disconnects you
blocks.text: If you clear the site's data (e.g., browser settings → clear data), the folder handle is wiped. Your data on disk is untouched. You just have to pick the folder again next time you open ResearchOS.
Setting up a shared lab folder

If multiple people in your lab should share one folder, put the folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud, and follow Shared Lab Accounts . The critical step is making sure the folder is always available offline on every member's laptop.

entries.title: Creating a User
entries.summary: Each person in the folder picks a unique username. Their data lives under users/<username>/.
entries.blurb: Pick a username, set an optional password.

blocks.text: Each person in the folder picks a unique username. Their data lives under users/<username>/.

blocks.alt: The user-picker screen with the + Create New User button, the existing-user list, and the BeakerBot brand mark in the gradient pill at the top.
blocks.caption: The user-picker screen, shown right after you connect a folder. (Screenshot pending recapture; the Lab Mode button shown in older captures has been removed and the BeakerBot brand mark added. Use ?wikiCapture=1 when recapturing.)

blocks.text: What "user" means here
ResearchOS has no central account system. Each "user" is a named folder inside in your data directory, with their own projects, tasks, methods, and notes underneath.

In this model, the folder is the lab . Anyone with access to the same folder can pick a username on the user-picker and become a member, no invite step needed. If a folder is private to you, you're a lab of one. If a folder is shared via OneDrive / Google Drive / Dropbox / iCloud, everyone with read-write access to that folder is in the same lab. See Shared Lab Accounts for the cloud setup.
Creating a user

blocks.title: What happens right after Create
blocks.text: Brand-new users land in BeakerBot's welcome tour, a short guided walkthrough on your real account. Plan on roughly five to ten minutes, depending on which features you opt into. See Welcome Tour (BeakerBot) for the full walkthrough, or skip it and explore on your own.
Optional password

If you share a laptop, or you're storing the folder in a shared cloud, you can set a per-user password. Go to Settings → Security → Set Password . The password is PBKDF2-hashed and stored in . You can also set or change a password directly from the user-picker by hovering a user row and clicking the padlock icon that appears.

blocks.title: Password recovery
blocks.text: Forgot your password? Delete from your folder (in Finder or Explorer) and the password gate goes away. Your data is untouched. This means a password isn't real security, it's a deterrent on a shared machine.

blocks.title: PI accounts always have a password
blocks.text: The optional password above is for members and solo accounts. A PI (an account flagged ) is the exception, a PI must set a password during account setup before they can finish, and it's enforced at login. So there's never a PI account with no password behind it. See Edit session and password .
Switching users
Click your avatar in the top-right of the header, then Switch user . Pick another user from the list. Your view and data reload to that user's namespace.
Main user

One user can be marked as the main user . They get a star badge on their avatar in the picker and are auto-selected as the default on the next visit. To set a main user, hover any user row on the picker and click the star (outline) icon that appears on the right. The main badge persists between sessions.
Renaming or deleting a user

To rename a user, hover their row on the user-picker and click the pencil (edit) icon. The row switches to an inline input with the current username pre-filled. Edit the name and press Enter or click the green checkmark to save. Press Escape or the red X to cancel. The same username rules apply (letters, numbers, underscores only). The rename renames the directory on disk and updates all internal references.

To delete a user, hover their row and click the trash icon. A two-step confirmation dialog appears with an optional "Archive data before deletion" checkbox (checked by default). The archive step downloads a ZIP of the user's folder before the directory is removed.

entries.title: Demo Mode
entries.summary: An in-browser preview of ResearchOS, seeded with a fake yeast lab. No folder picker, nothing to install, edits disappear on reload.
entries.blurb: Try ResearchOS in the browser with a seeded yeast lab.

blocks.text: An in-browser preview of ResearchOS, seeded with a fake yeast lab. No folder picker, nothing to install, edits disappear on reload.

Open (e.g., research-os-xi.vercel.app/demo , the canonical public URL as of this writing; check the project site for the current link if it has moved) and you land straight inside ResearchOS as a researcher named alex , with a populated lab to click around in. Nothing touches your disk and the browser never asks for a folder.
What you'll see

The Home page opens with four projects, all prefixed DEMO: so it's obvious nothing here is real research. Each project has experiments on the Gantt, methods in the library, a couple of PCR protocols, a funded purchase list, and a few results pages with lab notes. Other seeded users have shared records across alex's projects, so the comment threads, the lab announcements, and the cross-user views all have real content the moment you arrive.

Bare stays at in the URL bar and renders the Home page. Click any nav link (Gantt, Methods, Calendar) and the URL switches to the regular app path ( , ). Demo state follows you through those navigations via a sticky session flag, so the watermarked data and the demo affordances persist across every route. Deep links like also work: the app installs the fixture first, then redirects to so the shareable link lands the visitor on the right view.
Who's in the demo lab
Four seeded users are available from the user-picker. Knowing who is who matters because the lab-head and shared-records walkthroughs depend on signing in as the right researcher.

items: alex (member). The default sign-in. Owns all four DEMO: projects and is the user you start as on .
morgan (member). Shares one project with alex and drops comments into alex's threads, so any view that filters by shared records shows real cross-user activity.

Dr. Mira Castellanos (account_type ). The PI of the demo lab. Mira is the user to pick when exploring the lab-head walkthrough, the lab inbox, the PI audit log, the announcements, and the flag-for-review notifications. She owns the orange (#f97316) user color in the comment threads.

Dr. Sam Whitley (member, archived). Sam was archived by mira on 2026-03-15 and exists to demo the user-archiving feature. His historical comments and methods are still visible across the lab, but he no longer shows up in assignment pickers.

blocks.text: Edits are temporary

Everything you do in (dragging a Gantt bar, editing a lab note, adding a task, dropping in an image) stays in this browser tab. There's no disk write, no upload, no account. Refresh the tab and the demo resets to its seed state. Good for kicking the tires, not for real research.
Dates always feel current

Every demo experiment, goal, and event gets its start and end date rebased on the fly so the demo's "today" matches your real today. Visit the demo this week and upcoming experiments stay upcoming, the Gantt scrolls to the current week, and the calendar shows events around now. Visit it again in a month and the whole timeline slides forward to match.

Method history, PCR protocol creation dates, and lab note timestamps stay put, because they read as history rather than schedule. The rebase is also idempotent, so visiting twice in the same day doesn't accumulate drift.
Reading the docs from inside the demo
Two floating affordances stay with you across every route inside the demo, regardless of whether the URL is a path or the equivalent live app path ( , , and so on).

In the bottom-right corner, a darker pill-shaped Read the docs button (with a small upward-right arrow glyph) shows up whenever the current view has a matching wiki page. Click it on and you land on the Methods wiki entry, ready to read about what you just clicked. The button silently hides on views without a docs page yet. Browser-back puts you straight back inside the demo with your state intact, because the sticky demo flag survives the trip to .

The mirror move works from the wiki side. Feature pages with a demo-able view embed an amber inline call-out (the component) that drops you into for that view. Each call-site picks its own label, so you'll see variants like Try the Gantt view , Try methods in the demo , Try the Workbench , and Open the demo and try Lab Notes depending on which feature page you're reading. The click installs the fixture and lands you on the live editor with the demo's seeded protocols already loaded.
Leaving the demo

A floating amber Leave Demo button lives in the bottom-right and follows you across every route while the demo flag is set. It's undismissable, so you never lose the exit. Clicking it opens a dialog titled Leave the demo? with two choices:

items: The amber Leave demo button at the top resets all demo state in this tab and drops you on the connect-folder screen, ready to pick a real folder (or start a new one). Demo edits are not saved to disk anywhere, so they go away with the reload. If you opened from a tab that was already connected to a real folder, the original folder is restored automatically.
The smaller Keep exploring the demo link at the bottom just closes the dialog if you clicked Leave Demo by mistake.

blocks.alt: The Leave the demo dialog with a primary Leave demo button and a smaller Keep exploring link below.
blocks.caption: The Leave Demo dialog. Confirm to reset and return to the folder picker, or keep exploring.

blocks.text: Three ways to try the app

items: (this page). Fastest. Open the URL, click around, no setup. Edits are temporary and vanish on reload. Best for a first look or for sharing a link with a labmate.

The "Or download as a starter folder" link. On the connect-folder screen there's a small text link under the big Explore demo in browser button. It downloads with the same seeded yeast lab. Unzip it, point ResearchOS at the unzipped folder, and your edits persist on disk for as long as you keep the folder around.

Connect a real folder. For actual research work. See Connecting Your Folder for the picker flow, and Shared Lab Accounts for putting that folder in OneDrive / Drive / Dropbox / iCloud so your whole lab shares it.

blocks.title: Every name in the demo is invented
blocks.text: The demo lab is full of fake-on-purpose labels: , , , and so on. If you decide to export the demo as a starter folder, rename the projects before you start filing real data in there so nothing reads as a tutorial leftover later.

entries.title: Exporting from LabArchives
entries.summary: ResearchOS imports LabArchives notebooks by reading the Offline Notebook ZIP that LabArchives exports. This page walks you through producing that ZIP.
entries.blurb: Generate the Offline Notebook ZIP that ResearchOS imports.

blocks.text: ResearchOS imports LabArchives notebooks by reading the Offline Notebook ZIP that LabArchives exports. This page walks you through producing that ZIP.
Why an offline ZIP

LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments. ResearchOS doesn't talk to LabArchives in real time, and it doesn't write anything back to your LabArchives account. The hand-off in between is a single file, the Offline Notebook ZIP , that LabArchives generates on demand and emails you when it's ready.

Once you have that ZIP downloaded, the ResearchOS importer reads it directly. You don't unzip it, and you don't need ResearchOS to be signed in to LabArchives. The next page over, Import from LabArchives , covers what the importer does with the file.

blocks.title: LabArchives may change their UI
blocks.text: LabArchives occasionally renames menu items or moves them between toolbars. If a label below doesn't match what you see on screen, look for the same underlying concept: you're asking LabArchives to produce an Offline Notebook export of a single notebook. Other names you might see for the same action include Create Offline Notebook , Export Notebook , and Backup Notebook .
Generate the offline ZIP
What's inside the ZIP
You don't need to open the ZIP yourself, but it helps to know what's in there:

items: One HTML file per notebook page, holding the page title, every entry on that page, and the entry text.
A subdirectory of attachments for each page, where image uploads and other bundled files sit alongside the HTML.
A top-level manifest that describes the notebook tree, the export timestamp, and who triggered the export.

blocks.text: Some inline images live in LabArchives' cloud as URLs rather than bundled bytes. The Offline Notebook ZIP keeps the URL but not the image itself for those. ResearchOS handles that on import through a separate Fetch images step, which the import page covers.

blocks.title: One ZIP can be re-imported later
blocks.text: ResearchOS's importer is idempotent against the same ZIP. If you re-run the import on the same file, pages you've already imported are skipped. If you export a fresher ZIP from LabArchives later and run it through, only the new pages land. See Re-running the import for the details.
Up next
With the ZIP downloaded, head to Import from LabArchives to walk through the ResearchOS-side wizard. You'll pick the format, drop the ZIP, preview what's inside, map folders to projects, and apply.

entries.title: User archiving
entries.summary: When a lab member leaves (graduates, rotates out, switches projects), archiving their account is the right move. Archiving hides them from active views but keeps every byte of their data on disk and in the lab's history. They drop out of the login picker, member workload, and lab activity feeds; the records they wrote stay readable to anyone with permission.
entries.blurb: Hide a former member from active views while preserving their data.

blocks.text: When a lab member leaves (graduates, rotates out, switches projects), archiving their account is the right move. Archiving hides them from active views but keeps every byte of their data on disk and in the lab's history. They drop out of the login picker, member workload, and lab activity feeds; the records they wrote stay readable to anyone with permission.

blocks.alt: The LabRoster table in Settings with three members listed and an Archive button revealed on hover for one of the rows.
blocks.caption: The LabRoster in Settings, Lab Mode tab. Hover a member row to surface the Archive action.

blocks.text: Archive vs. delete
The two paths are different on purpose:

items: Archive hides the user from active surfaces while preserving their on-disk data. Tasks they own, notes they wrote, purchases they logged, all stay where they are. Anyone with read access still sees them. The archived user just stops appearing in the picker, the member workload widget, and the active member roster.

Delete removes the user's folder from disk entirely. Their data goes with them. The delete affordance lives on the user picker (a trash icon on each row, documented in Creating a user ) and is the right tool only when you genuinely want the data gone (a test account, a duplicate).

blocks.text: Archive is reversible. Unarchive a member and they reappear on every surface as if they never left. Delete is permanent (modulo any archive ZIP you chose to download at delete time).
Where the affordance lives

Archiving is a PI soft-write. Open Settings -> Lab Mode tab and the LabRoster section lists every member. Hover any row to reveal an Archive button on the right. The first archive in a fresh session prompts for the PI password (see Edit session and password ); subsequent archives during the same 5-minute window run without re-prompting.
What happens on archive
The action does six things atomically:

items: Sets on the member's metadata entry.
Hides the member from the user picker by default (see filter control below).
Drops the member from member-workload, lab-activity, and recent-activity widgets on the Lab Overview.
Keeps their records (tasks, notes, purchases) readable to anyone who already had access. No permissions are stripped retroactively.
Keeps their comments and announcements visible in their original places, with an archived badge next to their name.
Writes an row to _pi_audit.json recording the actor, target, and timestamp.

blocks.text: Bringing an archived member back

At the top of the LabRoster section is a Show archived toggle. Flip it on and archived members reappear in the table with a muted style and an Unarchive button instead of Archive. One click (still gated by the edit session) restores them on every surface, and the audit row this time is .

Archived members are also filterable on the user picker. By default the picker hides them; a small Show archived checkbox at the bottom of the picker reveals them when you need to sign in as a former member to check their work.

blocks.title: The 'who archived me' question
blocks.text: Members never see an "archived" banner on their own account. If a PI archives someone who later signs back in, that person signs in normally and sees their data as they left it; only the cross-lab surfaces stop including them. The audit log is the source of truth for "why am I not showing up in the workload widget," readable by the PI from PI Actions.
When to archive

items: A graduate student finished and is no longer doing lab work, but their results and notes still need to be readable for the next person who picks up the project.
A rotation student wrapped up and you do not want them on the member-workload chart for upcoming planning.
An undergrad worked over the summer and is gone for the school year; archive now, unarchive when they return.

blocks.text: The right tool for "this account was a test that should not exist" is delete, not archive. Archive is for real members whose work you want to keep.

entries.title: Welcome Tour (BeakerBot)
entries.summary: BeakerBot's welcome tour is a guided walkthrough on your real account. It opens with a short setup Q&A, then drops you into a live tour that helps you create your first project, method, and experiment right in the app. Plan on five to fifteen minutes, depending on which optional surfaces you opt into.
entries.blurb: Guided walkthrough on your real account: setup Q&A, hands-on tour, cleanup grid.

blocks.text: BeakerBot's welcome tour is a guided walkthrough on your real account. It opens with a short setup Q&A, then drops you into a live tour that helps you create your first project, method, and experiment right in the app. Plan on five to fifteen minutes, depending on which optional surfaces you opt into.

blocks.alt: The opening screen of BeakerBot's welcome tour. A sky-blue beaker mascot waves on the left, a short two-sentence pitch sits to the right, and a Let's go button waits in the lower-right corner.
blocks.caption: The opening screen. BeakerBot greets you, sets expectations, and waits for Let's go.

blocks.text: What the welcome tour actually is

When you create a brand-new ResearchOS user, BeakerBot pops up to show you around. The tour runs on your real account, against your real local-first data folder. Anything BeakerBot creates (a sample project, a placeholder method, a first experiment) lands on disk just like normal work. At the end BeakerBot plays a short goodbye animation and auto-cleans the demo artifacts in the background, leaving you with your first real project.
Three things are true about every run:

items: The tour is one continuous experience inside the app, not a separate demo sandbox. There is no fake fixture you discard on close.

BeakerBot drives the pacing with a Got it, next or Next button on each step. You can also click Skip this step on any individual step, or the persistent I've got it from here link in the footer to jump straight to the goodbye screen.
Closing the tab mid-tour is safe. The next time you open the folder, BeakerBot offers Resume , Restart , or Discard .

blocks.title: If you remember the old seven-step modal
blocks.text: ResearchOS used to ship a different welcome wizard, a seven-step modal that asked which use-case you fit into and toggled tabs from a static map. That version has been retired. BeakerBot's tour now walks you through your own account instead of front-loading taxonomy questions.

blocks.title: This is not the 3-minute walkthrough on the picker
blocks.text: Quick disambiguation: the folder-picker screen (before you link a folder) shows a small Take the 3-minute walkthrough button next to BeakerBot in the upper-right. That is a separate, opt- in 4-beat modal that introduces ResearchOS at a high level (welcome, data security, folder choice, cloud provider). It runs before you commit to picking a folder, never writes to disk, and closes back to the picker. The longer tour described on this page is the in-product BeakerBot tour that auto-fires after you sign in to a fresh user account. See Connecting Your Folder for the picker-side walkthrough.
Who sees the tour
The tour auto-fires only when three conditions all hold:

items: The folder has no entry, or the file is empty.
There is no on disk for the active user, or the file has no and no field.
The session is not in a completed or skipped state. The dev flag overrides both completed and skipped states so the tour can be driven against fixture data.

blocks.text: Existing users never see the tour automatically. If you signed in before BeakerBot existed, your sidecar migrates quietly to the new schema with no auto-fire, no banner, no nag.
To run the tour after the fact, go to Settings > Onboarding and click Re-run tour . BeakerBot re-mounts in place immediately (no page reload) starting from the welcome screen.

blocks.alt: The Onboarding section of Settings showing the Re-run tour row with a sky-blue Re-run tour button on the right edge.
blocks.caption: The Re-run tour button in Settings. Clears completion state and re-mounts BeakerBot at the welcome screen without a page reload.

blocks.text: Phase 1: setup questions

The first few minutes are a short Q&A that BeakerBot uses to shape the rest of the tour and which tabs appear in your sidebar. Every question is a radio pick with a sensible default if you click Skip this step .
Q1: solo or lab?

The first call is whether you are flying solo (one user, your own account) or running a multi-person lab (everyone points their ResearchOS at the same shared folder). The pick determines whether the PI follow-up question (Q1c, below) fires and whether the Lab Links surface is called "Links" or "Lab Links".

blocks.alt: Q1 in the welcome tour showing two radio cards: Solo (selected) and Lab. BeakerBot stands in the upper-left in thinking pose.
blocks.caption: Q1 picks your account flavor. You can revisit this in Settings.

blocks.title: Q1 auto-skips when other users exist
blocks.text: When the lab folder already has other users in it (someone signed in before you), the wizard auto-skips Q1 and assumes Lab. The reasoning: if there is already a lab to join, you are joining a lab, not starting a solo workspace. Q1 still appears for the first user in a fresh folder so a single-person workflow stays a deliberate choice.
Q1c: are you the PI?

Conditional on Q1 = Lab. After picking Lab, BeakerBot follows up with a single binary question. Are you the PI, or are you a member? Picking PI sets to on this account; picking Member leaves it at the default. One person in the lab fills the PI slot. The picker badge in the login screen, the audit log, and the Lab Overview surface are all gated on the resulting . See PI .
Q2 through Q6: feature picks
Five questions about ResearchOS surfaces and preferences:

items: Q2: Will you track lab purchases? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q3: Want calendar feeds? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q4: Want a goal-tracking page? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q5: Want a phone companion for image inbox? (Yes / No / Maybe later)

Q6: AI Helper prompt size. ResearchOS can paste a system prompt into Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini so the assistant understands ResearchOS terminology. Full is the default. Medium and Minimal trim the prompt for smaller-context models. No or Maybe later skips the AI Helper tour cluster entirely.

blocks.text: These answers drive both which tabs appear in your sidebar and which conditional walkthrough clusters fire in Phase 2.
Q7: Links / Lab Links

The final setup question asks whether you want a tab for saving bookmarks: VPN links, lab calendars, freezer inventory spreadsheets, manuscript drafts, and so on. Each card holds a URL plus a label so you can jump straight to the resource.

The surface name is account-type-conditional. Solo accounts see Links ; lab accounts see Lab Links , a shared tab visible to everyone in the lab. Saying yes to Q7 adds the tab and fires the conditional walkthrough later in Phase 2.
Phase 2: walkthrough on your real account

This is where the tour starts touching real data. BeakerBot walks a universal sequence covering the major surfaces (Home, Project, Notifications, Workbench, the editor, Methods, Gantt, Settings, Search, and the wiki pointer), followed by four conditional clusters that fire only when the matching Q answer was yes .

The walkthrough was deliberately trimmed. Most clusters are pitched at awareness now. BeakerBot explains what a surface is for and why you would seek it out, lands one or two live examples so the page is not empty, then moves on. The aim is to leave you knowing the feature exists and what it solves, not to drill every button. A handful of beats still hand you a real action (create a project, create an experiment, wire a Gantt dependency) so the muscle memory sticks.
Universal sequence: major surface tour
Home and your first project

Projects are the top-level container that every experiment, method, and task hangs off, so the tour opens by making one. These two beats are hands-on. You click and type, because there is no better way to learn the create flow than to run it once.
Project overview

The project page is where every experiment, method, and task you attach to a project comes back together in one view. The Overview box at the top is yours to fill in (hypothesis, motivation, why this project exists); the Results, Methods, and Activity tabs next to it surface automatically once you have something to show, and stay hidden while a project is still empty. This single beat introduces the Overview box, then hands you off to notifications.
Notifications

Two surfaces live in the top bar. The bell collects anything that needs your attention (reminders for upcoming work, updates from labmates, mentions on your writeups), and the inbox next to it collects files sent in from outside the app, like photos from a phone companion or shared attachments. This cluster was trimmed to two awareness beats. The old field-by-field demos for marking a row read and dismissing one were cut, since the inbox is self-explanatory once you know it exists.
Workbench experiment creation

Methods are the recipe; the Workbench is where you actually run them. Every experiment gets its own entry with space for notes, results, attached protocols, and files. This is the page you spend most of your time on, so the create flow stays hands-on across two beats.
Open the experiment, meet the Methods tab
The methods detour is set up with a single framing beat before the editor cluster, so you know where reusable protocols get pinned to a run before you go build one.
The editor (3 beats)

ResearchOS uses one editor everywhere, in project overviews, standalone notes, method writeups, and the experiment notes you are looking at now. It is inline-only. You just type and your markdown renders as you go, so there is no edit mode to toggle. The old markdown deep-dive (a primer plus cursor demos for bold, italics, headings, shortcuts, and image and file attachment) and the Focus Mode enter/exit demos were all cut once the editor became inline-only. Three awareness beats remain.
Workbench notes and lists (2 beats)

After the editor, BeakerBot introduces the standalone Notes and Lists panels on the Workbench. The cluster was collapsed to two explanation beats. The tool is friendly enough that you only need to know what notes and lists are, so the three create demos were cut.
Methods deep-dive (3 beats)

Methods are your reusable protocol library. Write a technique once here, then attach it to every experiment that uses it instead of rewriting the steps each time. The cluster was collapsed from five beats to three. BeakerBot asks what kind of technique you run, opens the New Method picker so you can see the catalog of purpose-built builders, then creates a plain markdown method as the fallback. The two builder demos that used to drive the PCR thermal-cycle editor and the LC gradient chart were cut. The picker still surfaces them for you to explore.
Method attachment (2 beats)

Now that a method exists, the tour returns to the experiment to pin it. These two beats reopen the experiment popup on its Methods tab and teach the mental model. Methods are the protocol template, variation notes are the per-run delta.
Gantt deep-dive
Six universal beats teach core Gantt mechanics. Lab accounts see an additional six-beat share-feature cluster.
Lab accounts only (gated on Q1 = lab). Six share-feature beats follow the universal arc.
Goals overview (gated on Q4 = yes). A single step fires after the share cluster and explains the Goals overlay on the Gantt toolbar.
Settings deep-dive (13 steps)

Settings is the last stop on the universal arc. BeakerBot opens with a narration beat that establishes scope (everything about the account, from appearance and visible tabs to integrations, the AI Helper prompt, and the re-run button), then walks two personalization beats on the Gantt toolbar, six Settings-page narration beats, and a four-beat AI Helper cluster.

blocks.title: settings-tour-calendar is retired
blocks.text: An earlier build had a beat here. It was retired because it told you to head over to the Calendar tab while the tour page-lock kept you on Settings, so it had nothing actionable to say. Calendar feeds are managed from the Calendar tab itself.
Search
search-demo. BeakerBot opens the Search tab and live-types a query that matches the experiment you created earlier. The highlighted result lands in the list.
Wiki pointer (2 beats)

The final universal cluster introduces the help icon in the top-right of the AppShell. It was collapsed from four beats to two. The two cursor demos that navigated into the wiki and back were cut for a single icon, and the click-and-return behavior folded into the icon-spotlight speech as awareness.
Conditional walkthroughs (Phase 2b)
Four conditional clusters fire after the wiki-pointer cluster. Each cluster gates on the matching Q answer being yes .

items: Purchases (Q2 = yes). An eight-step cluster in two phases. Phase 1 teaches on your empty page (intro, create-button click, form fill, autocomplete demo). Phase 2 warps into a read-only viewer over Alex's demo account to show the analytics surface, then navigates back.
Calendar (Q3 = yes). The step covers the inline calendar-feed subscribe flow. The new feed appears in the Calendar tab when you click Next.
Links / Lab Links (Q7 = yes). The step walks the bookmark tab, adding a card with a URL and label, and the difference between personal Links (solo) and shared Lab Links (lab).

blocks.title: The retired Lab Mode tour cluster
blocks.text: Lab Mode has been retired in favor of the per-user Lab Overview (PIs) and Home canvas (members). The dedicated PI walkthrough cluster that once ran here was also retired ahead of a future rebuild, so there is no separate Lab Overview tour today. A single step still runs at the end to wipe the BeakerBot fake user that was spawned during the Gantt share cluster. See Sharing and permissions for the data-side retirement story.
Terminal step: tour-goodbye
The final step is . BeakerBot says "You're set! Here's to many great experiments ahead." and presents a single Let's go button.
Clicking Let's go triggers the outro:

items: A full-screen overlay mounts. BeakerBot cheers and confetti fires (~1.8 s).
BeakerBot shifts to waving pose and translates off-screen (~1.8 s).
The overlay fades out (~0.8 s). The route lands on .
Auto-cleanup runs silently in the background, removing demo artifacts and leaving your first real project intact.
A small toast in the lower-right reads "Tour complete. Find BeakerBot again in Settings -> Onboarding." It auto-dismisses after 4 s.

blocks.text: Clicking I've got it from here at any earlier step skips the intervening steps and jumps directly to .
BeakerBot, the character

BeakerBot is the canonical ResearchOS mascot, a sky-blue chemistry beaker with pastel-rainbow liquid, dot eyes, and measurement-mark cheek dashes. The voice is funny and playful throughout. The tour draws from nine or more poses, chosen contextually:

items: Idle , the always-on baseline bob.
Waving , the welcome screen and the resume modal.
Thinking , head-tilt during setup Q1 to Q7 and the PI prompt.
Pointing , universal walkthrough steps where BeakerBot directs your attention to a UI element at eye level.
Pointing-up , steps where BeakerBot directs attention to the top bar (the wiki icon cluster).
Typing , steps where BeakerBot live-types into a form or field.
Typing-on-laptop , a one-hand typing variant used on notes and list creation beats.
Cheering , the tour-goodbye outro animation.
Bouncing , a ~650 ms burst on every step transition.

blocks.text: Behavior contracts
I've got it from here

A persistent link in the footer of every step. Click it, confirm in the sub-modal, and BeakerBot jumps to . The run gets recorded as a skip rather than a completion, so re-running from Settings still works.
Skip this step (individual)
Each step (after the intro) has a Skip this step link. If a later step depends on the artifact this step creates, BeakerBot silently creates a placeholder version with .
Mid-walkthrough close
Closing the tab partway through writes the current step plus every artifact created so far into . The next time you open ResearchOS with this folder, a small modal asks:

items: Resume , mount the tour at the saved step with every artifact and feature-pick intact.
Restart , wipe and so Q1 through Q7 run fresh, start at welcome.
Discard , set , clear resume state and feature picks. The tour exits. Settings re-run is the only path back.

blocks.alt: The Resume modal showing three buttons: a sky-blue Resume button on the right, a Restart button, and an outlined Discard button on the left. BeakerBot waves from the upper-left.
blocks.caption: The Resume modal. Fires on next open when wizard_resume_state is non-null and the saved step is past welcome.

blocks.text: Re-running from Settings
Clicking Re-run tour in Settings > Onboarding performs an inline that clears , , , , , , and in a single write. The controller then calls to re-mount the tour in place. No page reload.
How feature picks change which tabs you see
Your Q1 through Q7 answers determine the visible-tab set through two helpers in :

items: maps your picks to a canonical list of tab hrefs.
composes that list with the array in .

blocks.text: The rules:

items: Always visible: Home, Workbench, Methods, Experiments, Gantt, Search.
Lab Overview appears only when (the PI dashboard at ).
Purchases appears only when .
Calendar appears only when .
Goals appears only when .
Links / Lab Links appears only when . Solo accounts see the tab labeled Links ; lab accounts see Lab Links .

blocks.text: Settings can manually hide a tab that the picks would otherwise show. Settings cannot unhide a tab that the picks excluded. To get a hidden tab back, re-run the tour and flip the matching Q answer, or toggle it on directly in Settings > Onboarding > Visible tabs .

blocks.title: Power-user shortcut: ?wizard-preview=1
blocks.text: Developers and demo-givers can append to any URL to force-mount the tour against the current sidecar state. The session also activates when the sticky sessionStorage key is set to , which persists the preview mode across in-app navigations that strip the query string. Use either path together with to drive the tour against the wiki fixture for screenshot captures.
Dev affordances: the BeakerBot button

In development builds, a small BeakerBot button sits in the bottom-right floating cluster alongside the data-folder and switch-user controls. Clicking it opens a dropdown with three escape hatches for driving the tour without walking it from scratch. The whole control is gated on , so production builds drop it as dead code.
The three actions are:

items: Mount at step. Pick any v4 step ID from the dropdown (every node in , the full 78-step graph from through ) and click Mount wizard at this step . The orchestrator writes a resume_state pointing at your pick, flips the force-show flag on the current user's sidecar, and reloads. The tour re-mounts at the chosen step with every prior artifact intact. Useful for QA on a specific step body or for staging a screenshot capture at an arbitrary point in the flow.

Reset wizard state. Clears , , , , and on the current user's sidecar, then reloads. The tour fires from the intro on next mount, identical to a brand-new user. Faster than deleting the JSON by hand when iterating on step bodies.

Test-N sandbox. The third row, Show welcome wizard (creates Test user) , spawns a throwaway user (auto-incrementing N), force-shows the wizard on that user's sidecar, and swaps the active user. The sandbox user is real (it lives in and writes to disk), but it never touches the seen-once state on your primary account. Useful for capturing fresh-user screenshots or for stress-testing the wizard without disturbing the account you actually use.

blocks.title: Development builds only
blocks.text: The BeakerBot button never renders in production. It is also a no-op in demo and wiki-capture mode because the orchestrator is not mounted there. If you need to drive the tour in fixture mode, use the URL flag combo ( ) instead.
Where to go next

items: To re-run the tour or trim your visible tabs, see Settings .
For the Lab Overview dashboard that the PI cluster introduces, see Lab Overview .
For a hands-on tour against seeded data (no real folder needed), see /demo .
For how projects work after BeakerBot leaves, see Project Surface .

entries.title: Calendar Feeds
entries.summary: Overlay events from Google, Outlook, iCloud, or any public iCal URL on the ResearchOS calendar. Read-only, so you can plan around them without ResearchOS ever writing back to the source.
entries.blurb: Subscribe to Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendars via iCal URL.

blocks.text: Overlay events from Google, Outlook, iCloud, or any public iCal URL on the ResearchOS calendar. Read-only, so you can plan around them without ResearchOS ever writing back to the source.

blocks.alt: The Linked Calendars panel with a connected ICS subscription and the paste-a-URL form.

blocks.text: What it does

Each linked calendar shows its events on the Calendar page in the color you pick, sitting alongside ResearchOS tasks and events. Clicking an event opens a popup with the title, time, and any location or notes the source calendar published.

Linked calendars are read-only. ResearchOS fetches a public iCal (ICS) URL on a timer and overlays the events. It never writes back to Google, Outlook, or Apple. To edit an event, open it in its source app, and the change syncs into ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh.
Open the Linked Calendars panel
Go to the Calendar page. The Linked Calendars button sits top-right, next to + New Event . A small blue badge on the button counts how many subscriptions are currently turned on.
Add a calendar by ICS URL

blocks.title: webcal:// is fine
blocks.text: Paste a URL exactly as iCloud (or any other client) gives it to you. The server proxy rewrites it to automatically before fetching.
Where to copy the ICS URL from
Inside the panel, the Where do I find this URL? link expands a per-provider checklist that matches whichever Provider you have selected. The summaries below are the same steps in long form.
Google Calendar

items: Open in a desktop browser.
Click the gear -> Settings -> Settings for my calendars .
Pick the calendar you want to share in the sidebar.
Copy Secret address in iCal format for a private calendar, or Public address in iCal format if the calendar is already public.

blocks.text: Outlook / Microsoft 365

items: Open Outlook on the web -> Settings -> Calendar -> Shared calendars .
Under Publish a calendar , pick the calendar, choose Can view all details , and click Publish .
Copy the ICS link Outlook generates.

blocks.text: iCloud / Apple Calendar

items: Open the Calendar app on macOS, or sign in to in a browser.
Right-click the calendar in the sidebar -> Share Calendar -> Public Calendar .
Copy the share link. It starts with , which is fine to paste as-is.

blocks.text: Any other public iCal feed
Pick Other (any public iCal URL) in the Provider dropdown. That covers university course schedules, lab seminar series, conference timetables, sports schedules, anything that ends in or starts with .

blocks.title: Secret URLs leak your whole calendar
blocks.text: Google's Secret address in iCal format grants read access to every event title, time, and note in that calendar to anyone who has the URL. Treat it like a password (e.g., don't paste it into Slack, a public repo, or a shared notes doc). ResearchOS keeps the URL in your private data folder.
After a calendar is connected
Each subscription becomes a row in the Connected list at the top of the panel. The row shows:

items: The label you gave it, plus a small uppercase tag with the provider type.
The full ICS URL, truncated to one line.
A Last synced timestamp once ResearchOS has fetched the feed at least once.
A 5-swatch color strip on the left edge of the row. Click any swatch to recolor that calendar's events on the grid.
An On / Off checkbox to hide a calendar's events without removing the subscription.
A red Remove link to drop the subscription. A confirmation dialog reminds you that ResearchOS events aren't touched.

blocks.text: How often feeds refresh

ResearchOS refetches each feed at most once every 15 minutes per browser session. The fetch goes through a small server proxy (the same Vercel function that bypasses CORS for Google, Outlook, and iCloud), and the proxy caches the response on its own edge cache for another 15 minutes. Back-to-back page loads don't hammer the source calendar's servers as a result.

blocks.title: Stale-while-revalidate
blocks.text: The edge cache also serves the previous response while fetching a fresh copy in the background (stale-while-revalidate, up to 1 hour). In the worst case an event you just added could take up to 75 minutes to appear (15 min client window + 60 min stale window). Hard-refreshing the browser tab bypasses the client-side window but not the edge cache.
If you just added an event in the source calendar and want it on the ResearchOS grid right now, hard-refresh the browser tab or wait out the 15-minute window.
What URLs the server proxy will fetch

The proxy is the small server endpoint that fetches each ICS URL on your behalf, because browsers can't fetch directly (Google doesn't set the right CORS headers). The proxy refuses a few classes of URL outright so it can't be used to scan internal infrastructure:

items: Anything that isn't (so no , no , no ). Plain is rejected outright for security. The prefix is rewritten to first, so iCloud URLs still work.
and the loopback range .
Private IPv4 ranges: , , .
Link-local and the IPv6 equivalents ( , , ).
Responses that don't start with . This catches the case where a calendar share URL redirects to an HTML sign-in page instead of returning iCal text.

blocks.text: If a paste fails with one of those errors, double-check that the URL is the public ICS variant of the calendar (not the regular web-app share link).
Recurring events

Repeating events (e.g., a weekly seminar) are expanded into one chip per occurrence inside a ±2-year window around today. That keeps a 20-year-long daily recurrence from spawning ~7,300 chips on the grid. If you scroll the calendar more than two years backward or forward, repeating events from external feeds will stop showing up until you refresh the page from a closer date.
Want to edit linked events from ResearchOS?

Linked calendars are read-only by design. To make changes, open the event in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar). Your edit shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh. Earlier ResearchOS builds had an OAuth-backed two-way sync for Google and Outlook. That surface was removed (2026-05-14) in favor of keeping the integration simple and credential-free. The ICS subscription flow described here is the only supported path now.

entries.title: LabArchives
entries.summary: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks — and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.
entries.blurb: Import Offline Notebook ZIPs via the credential-free path; optional API path for online-only inline images.

blocks.text: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks — and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.
The shape of the integration

LabArchives and ResearchOS sit at opposite ends of a hand-off, not in a live sync. LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments; ResearchOS is where you bring those notebooks once you want them on disk in a structured form. There's no two-way connection, and ResearchOS never writes back to LabArchives.
Everything runs through one entry point under Settings -> LabArchives :

items: Import from LabArchives — the import wizard. You download an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives and feed it to the wizard. Every notebook page becomes a ResearchOS task; every folder becomes a project you can map to your existing project list. No credentials, works offline.

blocks.text: Form-A vs Form-B inline images

LabArchives stores inline images in two ways internally. The legacy path, called Form-A , embeds the image as a binary file inside the notebook page entry itself. The newer path, Form-B , uploads the image to LabArchives' cloud and leaves only a URL behind in the entry body.

When you generate an Offline Notebook export ZIP, only the Form-A images come along. The Form-B URLs are still in the entry text, but the image bytes were never bundled. In a typical recent notebook that's roughly half the inline images.

If you import the ZIP without fetching Form-B images, the wizard writes a placeholder for each one. Your notes still come across; the images at those URLs simply aren't there yet. You can recover them at any time using one of the three paths described below, or remove individual placeholders using the broken-image popup.
When images are recovered, inline images land in the task's folder and the markdown is rewritten to point at the local copy. File attachments (non-image uploads) land in .

blocks.title: Skip image fetch on the first pass
blocks.text: It's fine to skip image recovery during the initial import. Form-B images become placeholders you can clean up later. The broken-image popup in any imported note lets you handle them one at a time, and the per-task "Rehydrate images" panel lets you batch them all at once.
Exporting an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives
The ZIP that ResearchOS imports is LabArchives' built-in Offline Notebook export. Both the import and any image-recovery run depend on having this file first.
Running the import

items: Generate browser script — the DevTools-script path (see below).
Drop your own images — the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below).

blocks.title: What ends up on disk
blocks.text: Each notebook page becomes a directory under with a body, a folder for file attachments, a folder for inline images, and an for provenance. Tasks are marked complete, with and set to the page's newest entry timestamp.
Recovering Form-B images
Three paths exist to pull Form-B images into your notes. All three are credential-free: ResearchOS never asks for your LabArchives password.
Path 1: DevTools browser script

The Generate browser script tab (in the wizard's Fetch images step, or in the per-task Rehydrate panel) shows a self-contained JavaScript snippet you paste into the browser console while you're logged into LabArchives. The script:

items: Checks that it's running on — it refuses to do anything on other origins.
Fetches each missing image URL using your existing session cookies (no password, no API key — just the session the browser already has from your login).
Packages all successful fetches into a single ZIP and triggers one browser download.

blocks.text: Drop the downloaded ZIP into the Drop your own images tab (or drag it straight into the panel) and ResearchOS matches filenames automatically.

blocks.title: Security posture of the script
blocks.text: The script runs entirely in your browser. ResearchOS never sees the image bytes until you explicitly drop the resulting ZIP back into the wizard. The URLs are hard-coded into the script (no dynamic eval, no network round-trip back to ResearchOS). The flag only attaches cookies the browser already has from your LabArchives login — the script cannot fetch anything you couldn't load by clicking the image yourself.
Path 2: Manual folder or ZIP drop

If you already have the Form-B image files on disk (from a previous download, a screenshot batch, or an export from another tool), use the Drop your own images tab. Drop a folder or a containing the image files and ResearchOS matches them to the expected filenames automatically. No script needed.

This path also accepts the ZIP produced by the DevTools script above, which is why the two-tab flow is designed the way it is: generate and download in the DevTools tab, then drop the result in the manual tab.
Path 3: Per-image broken-placeholder popup
After import, any task that still has unresolved Form-B images shows broken image placeholders in the Lab Notes editor. Clicking a broken placeholder opens a small popup with three options:

items: Find on LabArchives — opens the original image URL in a new tab pointed at . If you're logged in, the image loads and you can right-click -> Save. Then use Replace from disk below, or drag the saved file onto the note.

Replace from disk — opens a file picker. Pick a local image file; ResearchOS writes it to , rewrites the markdown reference, and removes the entry from the task's sidecar so the popup doesn't re-appear on the next open.
Remove reference — deletes the broken placeholder from the markdown entirely. Use this when you don't have the image and don't need it.

blocks.title: Drag-and-drop shortcut
blocks.text: If you saved an image via "Find on LabArchives" and it filename-matches an outstanding placeholder, dragging it straight onto the note triggers the same recovery pipeline as the file picker — no need to open the popup first.

entries.title: Security
entries.summary: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.
entries.blurb: What stays on your computer, what briefly touches our server, and what we never collect.

blocks.text: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.
The claim

ResearchOS is built as a browser-only app that reads and writes a folder you pick on your own machine. Your notes, results, project and task data, images, attachments, and calendar subscriptions all stay in that folder. Nothing about your work is uploaded to a database we control, because there is no database we control. Two narrow proxy routes we wrote exist for browser CORS reasons and are documented below, but they are streams, not stores. Vercel Web Analytics adds one outbound destination, also documented below.
What stays on your computer

blocks.title: The short version
blocks.text: Open your data folder in Finder or Explorer. Every experiment, note, image, and attachment you have written is sitting there as a regular file you can read. ResearchOS writes to that folder and nowhere else. Quit the app and your data stays exactly where it is.

When you pick a data folder, ResearchOS reads and writes everything directly to that folder on your machine. The bytes never leave it. You can open the folder at any time in your operating system's file browser and see exactly what is there.
The folder holds:

items: Every experiment, lab note, and result you write.
Every image, PDF, and arbitrary attachment you drop into a note.
The JSON for projects, tasks, dependencies, methods, and PCR protocols.
Your calendar subscription URLs and the events they pulled in.
The optional PBKDF2-hashed password protecting your per-user profile.

blocks.text: {` / .gitignore app-managed, auto-appended for sensitive sidecars users/ _user_metadata.json cross-user color preferences + display names _global_counters.json cross-user id allocator public/ methods + PCR protocols shared across all users lab/ shared lab-account state (funding accounts, etc.) / settings.json user settings, default views, tab visibility _auth.json PBKDF2-hashed password (only if set) _counters.json id counters per entity type _shared_with_me.json inbound sharing pointers _calendar-feeds.json subscribed iCal URLs _notifications.json bell-dropdown rows projects/ .json one flat file per project tasks/ .json one flat file per task (the JSON carries fields; long-form text and attachments live under results/task- / below) methods/ .json reusable protocols pcr_protocols/ .json PCR programs and recipes lc_gradients/ .json LC gradient programs cell_culture_schedules/ .json plate_layouts/ .json notes/ .json shared lab notes results/task- / per-task long-form content + attachments notes.md your Lab Notes tab writeup results.md your Results tab writeup notes/Images/ images dropped into Lab Notes tab notes/Files/ files dropped into Lab Notes tab results/Images/ images dropped into Results tab results/Files/ files dropped into Results tab events/ .json native calendar events goals/ .json project goals lab_links/ .json link library entries purchase_items/ .json purchase orders inbox/Images/ photos awaiting routing`}
What briefly touches a server we operate

Two routes on the ResearchOS server are involved. The first is a CORS-bypass stream that exists because browsers refuse to talk directly to the upstream service. The second is an anonymous page-view ping. Neither ever sees the contents of your data folder.

items: Calendar feed sync. When you subscribe to an iCal URL (Google, Outlook, iCloud, a university calendar), the browser asks to fetch the iCal text from the upstream and stream it back. The subscription URL travels in the request header rather than in the URL query string, so it is never written to Vercel access logs. A 15-minute edge cache keeps repeated polls from hammering the upstream. We do not persist the URL or the contents.

Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, your browser sends an anonymous page-view beacon to Vercel telling them which route you visited. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies, no project names. Vercel sees your IP address (which they hash before storage per their privacy policy). The Settings -> Offline mode toggle disables it durably, the script tag is never injected when offline mode is on.

blocks.text: The calendar CORS-bypass proxy uses the most defensive shape we know how to write: HTTPS only, private-IP blocking, redirect re-validation, byte cap, timeout, content-type denylist, and per-IP rate limiting. The Vercel Analytics endpoint is a Vercel-owned script and beacon target, its posture is Vercel's, not ours. The route-defense code is in and if you want to read it line by line.
What we collect, and what we don't

We collect anonymous page-view pings via Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, anonymous beacons go to Vercel. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies. We use this to know which pages get used and which sit idle. Settings -> Offline mode disables it durably, the script is not injected when the toggle is on, and the toggle is read at component-mount time so the choice survives reloads.

We do not collect anything else. No Sentry, no Google Analytics, no Mixpanel, no PostHog, no Hotjar, no Datadog, no Amplitude. No background phone-home. No crash reporter. No content telemetry. Running against the repo will confirm only is present, and the network tab will confirm no other endpoints are contacted.

The Report an issue button does not auto-submit anything. When you click it, your browser opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. You see the body, edit it, and click Submit . Nothing happens until you do.

blocks.alt: The Report an Issue modal, showing the Type radio group, an editable Title field, an editable description, optional auto-attached error details, and the Cancel / Copy Link / Create GitHub Issue buttons at the bottom.
blocks.caption: The Report an Issue modal. You see the body, you edit the body, you choose whether to submit.

blocks.text: Honest limits worth knowing about
These claims hold, but they have implications worth understanding before you trust ResearchOS with anything sensitive.

blocks.title: Folder sharing means folder trust
blocks.text: If you share your data folder with a lab-mate over OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud, every byte in it is theirs to read. The per-user password gate stops accidental account-switching inside the app, not someone opening the folder in their own file browser.

blocks.title: Passwords aren't encryption
blocks.text: We hash account passwords with PBKDF2-SHA-256 (600,000 iterations) so a snooping co-located user can't trivially read them, but the data itself sits in plaintext on disk. Anyone with disk access has it. Turn on OS-level full-disk encryption (FileVault on macOS, BitLocker on Windows) if your laptop walks around.

blocks.title: Public hosting is opt-in, and the proxy is open
blocks.text: If you self-host ResearchOS on a public Vercel deploy with no auth gate, anyone on the internet can hit the calendar-feed proxy route (subject to rate limiting). This is not a data-exfiltration risk because the proxy has no access to your data folder, but it does mean someone could burn your Vercel function budget. Set for shared-state rate limiting on public deploys. The wiring is already in place.
How to verify it yourself
You don't have to take any of this on faith. There are two ways to confirm what the app is doing. The first is built into Settings and takes about thirty seconds.

blocks.title: The easy way: open Settings
blocks.text: ResearchOS ships three affordances inside Settings: The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English. The Offline mode toggle disables the two proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics script tag in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface. "Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.

items: The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English.
The Offline mode toggle disables the two proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics script tag in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface.
"Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.

blocks.text: Your browser already shows every network request the app makes. This is the audit-grade path for anyone who wants to see the bytes themselves.

items: holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked.
holds three small session-routing strings: the folder name plus its grant timestamp, the currently signed-in user, and (if you are a PI signed in) the primary account.

blocks.text: What we just fixed

ResearchOS went through an internal security audit on 2026-05-15. It closed one Critical finding (cross-site scripting via unsanitized markdown HTML across all 8 markdown rendering sites in the app) and several Important findings around the proxy routes, the LabArchives credential flow, and the in-app verification surface (data inventory, offline mode, storage hints). The audit report is checked into the repo as SECURITY_AUDIT.md , and the merge batch on sits around (audit doc) and (XSS sanitize + CSP).
If you find something that looks wrong, open a GitHub issue (the Report an issue button is the fastest path) or email the maintainer directly. We'd much rather hear about a problem early.
Atomic-write safety

A subtler kind of data loss has nothing to do with the network and everything to do with timing. If an app is halfway through writing a file when the tab closes, the laptop sleeps, or the process crashes, a naive implementation can leave the file zero-byte or truncated, and the good version you had a second ago is gone. ResearchOS is built so that cannot happen.

Every save writes to a temporary file first, lets that file finish landing durably on disk, and only then atomically moves it into place over the real file. The move is the kind of operation that either fully happens or does not happen at all, with no in-between state on disk. So if a crash interrupts a save, the worst case is that your previous good version survives untouched. You can never be left with a half-written or empty file in place of your data. The implementation is in if you want to read the exact sequence.

blocks.title: The worst case is your last good save
blocks.text: A torn write can only ever leave the old file contents intact, never a corrupt or empty one. The atomic move is the whole point. Either the new version replaces the old one cleanly, or the old one is still there.
Tested on every commit

A trust claim is only as good as the thing that keeps it true over time. The whole app is gated by automated tests that run on every commit and every pull request to : linting, a full TypeScript typecheck, the Vitest unit and integration suite with coverage, and Playwright end-to-end tests in a real browser. The workflow lives in . If any of those gates fail, the change does not ship. That same machinery is what keeps the scientific calculations honest, which is its own page: Method validation explains how every sequence and lab calculation is re-checked against the reference tools the field trusts, on every commit.

entries.title: Shared Lab Accounts
entries.summary: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.
entries.blurb: Run one ResearchOS folder across a whole lab.

blocks.text: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.

blocks.title: Want to feel out the shared model first?
blocks.text: Open Demo Mode for a no-setup preview. It seeds two researchers (alex and morgan) plus their PI (Mira) into one shared folder, so cross-user data and the PI rollup are already wired up for you to click around in.
How it works

Put your ResearchOS folder inside a cloud-synced folder (e.g., OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud). Every lab member points ResearchOS at that same folder on their own computer. Inside the folder, each member picks a different username, so their data lives at , , and so on.
The cloud provider handles syncing files between machines. ResearchOS has no idea any of this is happening, it just reads and writes JSON files like normal.

blocks.title: The one rule: keep the folder available offline
blocks.text: ResearchOS NEEDS to read and write the folder directly on disk. If your cloud provider keeps the folder "online only" (i.e., a placeholder file that downloads on demand), writes will fail or be silently dropped. EVERY member needs to flip the "always keep local" switch for the lab folder on EVERY laptop they use ResearchOS from.
Setup steps (every member runs these once)
Pick your cloud provider
Verify the folder is local
Before you start using ResearchOS, open the shared folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows) and confirm:

items: Files show as fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon, no "online only" badge, no "available when online" tag).
You can open a sample file with the network disconnected.
The folder's "Size on disk" in its properties dialog is non-zero and roughly matches the "Size".

blocks.title: The PI sees the lab roll-up
blocks.text: Once everyone is set up, the PI sees a customizable dashboard at with widgets for cross-lab activity, purchases, member workload, announcements, and more. See Lab Overview . If you only want one labmate's work surfaced on your own Home page (rather than the whole lab), they can share an individual project or task with you instead; see Home & Projects .
Pitfalls to avoid

items: Don't put the folder in two different cloud providers. Pick one. Stacking OneDrive and Dropbox on the same folder corrupts JSON files when both try to write.
Don't use the same username on two laptops. If Sarah signs in as on her laptop and also on a shared lab computer at the same time, the two ResearchOS sessions will overwrite each other's files.
Don't edit files outside ResearchOS while the app is open. The app caches data in memory, so external edits won't be picked up until you reload the page.

entries.title: Box
entries.summary: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.
entries.blurb: Box Drive with Make Available Offline.

blocks.text: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.
Install Box Drive

Download Box Drive from box.com/resources/downloads . You need the desktop client running. The web interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

blocks.title: Box Drive, not Box Sync
blocks.text: Box Sync is the older client and has been retired. Make sure you're installing Box Drive . Box Drive shows every folder you have access to as a virtual filesystem and downloads files on demand, whereas Box Sync mirrored selected folders to disk. The instructions below assume Box Drive.
Share the lab folder
Make it Available Offline (every member, every laptop)

Box Drive streams files on demand by default, so the folder shows up as a placeholder until you open something. ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still cloud-only breaks. You need to mark the lab folder as available offline on every laptop.

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for Box folders. This is normal; Box Drive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: Enterprise policy can block Make Available Offline
blocks.text: Some Box Enterprise admins disable Make Available Offline for compliance reasons. If you right-click the folder and don't see the option, or it's greyed out, ask your IT admin to enable offline access for your account. Without it, ResearchOS will hit empty placeholder files instead of real content.
Where the folder lives on disk

items: Windows : under (e.g. ).
macOS : under on recent macOS versions, or on older installs.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls

items: Files revert to cloud-only. If a colleague opens the shared folder's properties and chooses Free up space , the folder switches back to streaming and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Make Available Offline .

Conflict files. If two members edit the same file while offline, Box creates a copy named next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

Collaborator vs. owner storage. Shared folders on Box count against the folder owner's quota, not each collaborator's. Pick someone with enough quota (usually the lab's primary Box account) to own the folder.

Single sign-on re-auth. University Box accounts on SSO occasionally drop the Box Drive session and stop syncing without an obvious error. If files stop updating, open Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon and confirm you're still signed in.

items: Installing Box Drive (Box Support)
Making files and folders available offline with Box Drive (Box Support)
Inviting collaborators to a folder (Box Support)

entries.title: Dropbox
entries.summary: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
entries.blurb: Smart Sync set to Local.

blocks.text: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
Install Dropbox
Download the desktop app from dropbox.com/install . You need the desktop client running. The web interface alone won't expose Dropbox as a folder ResearchOS can read.
Share the lab folder
Set Smart Sync to Local (every member, every laptop)

blocks.title: Smart Sync isn't on every plan
blocks.text: Smart Sync is included on Dropbox Plus, Family, Professional, and Business plans. On the free Basic plan, all files are local by default, so you can skip the Smart Sync step entirely. Just watch your storage quota.

blocks.title: Or keep all of Dropbox local
blocks.text: If you're on a paid plan and want every Dropbox folder to stay on disk by default, open the Dropbox menu-bar / tray icon → Preferences -> Sync -> New folder default and set it to Local . Existing folders keep their current setting; only newly-added folders inherit the new default. Pick this if you have multiple Dropbox folders besides the lab folder that all need to be always-local.
Common pitfalls

items: Default to Online-only. Some Business plans default new shared folders to Online-only . Check the team admin settings if Smart Sync keeps reverting.
Selective Sync vs. Smart Sync. Selective Sync hides the folder entirely from your disk, while Smart Sync keeps a placeholder. You want Smart Sync set to Local , not Selective Sync excluded.

LAN sync conflicts. If two members on the same network edit the same file in quick succession, Dropbox can produce "conflicted copy" files. These appear next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

blocks.text: Dropbox documentation

items: How to use Smart Sync (Dropbox Help)
Share files and folders (Dropbox Help)

entries.title: Google Drive
entries.summary: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.
entries.blurb: Drive for desktop in Mirror mode.

blocks.text: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.
Install Drive for desktop

Download Drive for desktop for Windows or macOS. The browser interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.
Share the lab folder
Mark the lab folder Available offline (every member, every laptop)

ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still in Stream mode breaks. Most labs don't want the whole Drive switched to Mirror (your unrelated personal files, downloads, photos all get pulled local). Instead, mark just the lab folder as available offline.
Drive doesn't carry the "available offline" flag across machines, so every member has to do this on every laptop they use ResearchOS from.

blocks.title: Or switch the whole Drive to Mirror
blocks.text: If you want all of Drive locally (not just the lab folder), open Drive for desktop's Preferences → My Drive → Mirror files and confirm. Drive will download everything you have in My Drive over the next few minutes or hours depending on size. Pick this if you don't mind the extra disk use or you have other folders besides the lab folder that need to stay local.
Where the folder lives on disk

items: Windows : usually mounted as drive or under .
macOS : under or shown as a volume on the desktop.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls

items: Stream mode by accident. If files in the lab folder show a cloud icon or take seconds to open, the folder isn't marked offline yet. Right-click the lab folder and pick Available offline (or switch your whole Drive to Mirror if you prefer that).
"Shared with me" is not synced. A shared folder doesn't appear in your local Drive folder until you Add shortcut to Drive . Without that, ResearchOS has nothing to point at.
Workspace admins sometimes lock Mirror mode off. Check with your IT admin if Drive for desktop only shows the Stream option.

Share folders in Google Drive (Google Help)

entries.title: iCloud Drive
entries.summary: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.
entries.blurb: Keep Downloaded on macOS.

blocks.text: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.

blocks.title: macOS only, realistically
blocks.text: iCloud Drive technically runs on Windows via the iCloud for Windows app, but the experience is rough and Apple-specific permission quirks can break ResearchOS's folder access. If your lab has a mix of Mac and Windows users, we recommend OneDrive or Google Drive instead.
Enable iCloud Drive
On macOS, open System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud → iCloud Drive . Make sure it's turned on, and that the Desktop & Documents Folders sub-option is enabled if you plan to put the lab folder under .
Share the lab folder
Keep Downloaded (every member, every Mac)

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for iCloud Drive folders. This is normal; iCloud needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: Or keep all of iCloud Drive local
blocks.text: If you'd rather not have macOS offloading anything in iCloud, open System Settings -> Apple ID -> iCloud and turn off Optimize Mac Storage . Every file in your iCloud Drive stays on disk from then on. Reasonable if you have plenty of disk space and other iCloud folders that also need to be always-local; the per-folder route above is the lighter option.
Common pitfalls

items: Optimize Mac Storage (System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud) will re-offload files macOS decides you haven't used recently. Keep Downloaded overrides this per folder, so make sure it's set on the lab folder, not just on individual files.
Permissions on shared collaborations are tracked by Apple ID. If someone's share is revoked, ResearchOS will see empty or unreadable files. Reaccept the share via the email link.
Hidden placeholder files appear if the file is online-only. If you see instead of , the file isn't local. Run Keep Downloaded again.

blocks.text: Apple documentation

items: Use iCloud Drive on your Mac (Apple Support)
Collaborate on folders with iCloud Drive (Apple Support)

entries.title: OneDrive
entries.summary: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
entries.blurb: Files On-Demand → Always keep on this device.

blocks.text: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
Get OneDrive

items: Windows 10/11 : pre-installed. Sign in via Settings → Accounts.
macOS : download from microsoft.com/onedrive/download .

blocks.text: Share the lab folder
Make it Always keep on this device (every member, every laptop)

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow on Windows
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open on Windows for OneDrive folders. This is normal; OneDrive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: OneDrive Personal vs. Business / SharePoint
blocks.text: Shared folders sometimes show up as SharePoint libraries or in a separate "Shared with me" section. Microsoft's Add shortcut to My files button is the cleanest way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDrive tree, where Always keep on this device works reliably.

blocks.title: Or keep all of OneDrive local
blocks.text: If you'd rather have your whole OneDrive on disk (not just the lab folder), click the OneDrive icon in the menu-bar / tray → Settings -> Sync and backup -> Advanced settings -> Files On-Demand and switch to Download all OneDrive files now . Pick this if you have other OneDrive folders you also need available offline.
Common pitfalls

items: Storage Sense on Windows can auto-unpin files you haven't opened recently (i.e., move them back to cloud-only). Open Settings → System → Storage → Storage Sense and either disable the "content not opened in N days" option for the ResearchOS folder, or turn Storage Sense off entirely.
Cloud-only re-tag happens if you right-click and choose Free up space by accident. The folder reverts to cloud-only and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Always keep on this device .
Mac users : confirm the option says "Always keep on this device" (Files On-Demand). The older OneDrive Mac client stored files locally by default, but newer versions don't.
